                                                 MIL-STD-881F
                                                 APPENDIX K

         a.   Integration and test efforts that can be associated specifically with the equipment
              (hardware/software)

          K.5.1.3 Program Management. Program management (PM) includes the resources necessary to manage,
direct, and control all effort contributing to the development, production of custom and commercial off-the-shelf
(COTS) procurements and integration. Additionally, it includes training related items used to facilitate instruction
through which personnel will learn to operate and maintain the system and elements of the system. Data related
items are also captured here, such as the deliverable data required to be listed on a Contract Data Requirements
List (CDRL).

         Includes, for example:
         a. Project management
         b. Business management (includes business operations., scheduling, accounting, and finance)
         c. Project control and planning
         d. Configuration management
         e. Contract/subcontract management
         f. Security management
         g. Delivered data, including, for example:
              i. Technical publications and technical manuals
             ii. Cost and Schedule Reporting (CPR, CFSR, IMS, WBS, etc.)
            iii. Training plans, manuals, guides
         h. Data management including data repository and associated drafting and clerical effort to maintain
              master, Government approved, and Government owned documents
         i. Administration
         j. Product effectiveness
         k. Training services to include: course development, material preparation, and conducting initial
              training

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Program management efforts that can be associated specifically with the equipment
             (hardware/software)

K6. DEFINITIONS OF COMMON ELEMENTS APPLICABLE TO INFORMATION SYSTEMS /
DEFENSE BUSINESS SYSTEMS ONLY.

        Elements that are specifically unique to Information Systems/Defense Business Systems are defined in
K.6. The Information Systems/Defense Business Systems (IS/DBS) commodity has unique application of change
management, data management, operational infrastructure/site activation, and interim operations and support (Pre-
IOC). For the remaining Common Elements, reference paragraph K.3 Definitions of Common Elements.

        K.6.1 Change Management. Change management refers to the broad process for managing organizational
change. Change management encompasses planning, oversight or governance, project management, testing, and
implementation.

         K.6.2 Data Management. This element includes data stewardship, quality management, governance, and
data security management in support of the IS/DBS.

        K.6.3 Operational Infrastructure/Site Activation by Site 1...n (Specify). The costs associated with
deploying the IS/DBS solution at the user site(s). This should cover only those efforts that are incurred at the
implementation site. Any upfront effort involved with designing/engineering the solution for a particular site
should be included in under client-side site development. Any effort related to redesign of the solution once
implementation has begun should be captured here.

         Note: This element will also include the real estate, construction, conversion, utilities, and equipment to
provide all facilities required to house, service, and launch the IS/DBS solution at the user sites.


                                                        223

                           Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                         Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                  MIL-STD-881F
                                                  APPENDIX K


         K.6.3.1 Initial Hardware Procurement. Commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) hardware required for various
types of facilities supporting end-user equipment, cybersecurity equipment, and IT infrastructure and enterprise
software equipment.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Processing equipment (servers, CPUs, etc.)
         b. Network/communications
         c. Storage
         d. Security/crypto
         e. UPS
         f. Other/ancillary equipment

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Development/test hardware (captured with the prime mission product)

        K.6.3.2 Initial Software License Procurement. COTS software purchased for the various site types. This
would include the purchase of software licenses related to the IS/DBS solution.

         Includes, for example:
         a. End-user software licenses
         b. Cybersecurity software licenses/services
         c. IT infrastructure and enterprise services software
         d. Other 1...n (Specify)

        K.6.3.3 Initial Software Release (Pre-IOC) Modification/Enhancement. This element includes the effort
to modify or enhance the software developed for the system to meet the site requirements necessary to support the
IS/DBS.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Routine fixes/deficiency correction
         b. Deployment independent verification and validation
         c. Installation and test

          K.6.3.4 Site Activation. This element includes the costs associated with shipping costs to the site, site
survey, site preparation, equipment installation, equipment integration, test, and checkout to allow implementation
of the IS/DBS solution. This should cover only those efforts that are incurred at the implementation site. Any effort
related to redesign of the solution once implementation has begun should be captured here.

         K.6.3.4.1 Data Migration. This element includes the effort for translating data from one format to another.
This should cover only those efforts that are incurred at the implementation site. Also include any expense
associated with the transition of data from the legacy systems to the IS/DBS solution. Use lower levels to identify
individual legacy systems.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Data translation
         b. Data cleansing
         c. Data loading

         Excludes, for example:
         a. External system interface development

          Note: Data migration is necessary when an organization decides to use a new computing systems or
database management system that is incompatible with the current system. Typically, data migration is performed
by a set of customized programs or scripts that automatically transfer the data.


                                                         224

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                  MIL-STD-881F
                                                  APPENDIX K

          K.6.3.4.2 User Training. This element represents the effort involved with training the users of the
implemented IS/DBS solution at the user sites. The primary cost captured here will be the labor costs of instructors
to train users on the new system and business processes required to operate within the new system(s). Any costs
incurred as a result of revising the training courses and/or materials once implementation has begun should also be
captured here.

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Any upfront costs associated with training the trainers, as well as course development and material
             costs that are not incurred as a result of circumstances encountered at the user sites

         K.6.3.4.3 User Documentation. This element is documentation on how systems are set up and used. This
includes practices, procedures, and set up information. It is maintained by the application system users who are
responsible for the operation of the application system.

          K.6.3.4.4 Management/Engineering Support. This element captures those support elements that are
attributable only to specific user/implementation sites. This would include systems engineering/program
management effort associated with a specific site, as well as any system test and evaluation specific to particular
user sites.

           K.6.3.4.5 Site Installation, Test and Checkout. This element provides the support and services required to
install, test, and checkout the information system/defense business system at the site and ensure it conforms to the
requirements for site activation.

        K.6.3.5 Interim Operations and Support (Pre-IOC). The effort required to operate, maintain, and support
the IS/DBS completed prior to initial operating capability.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Help Desk support
         b. System Database Administration
         c. Installation, Test, and Checkout
         d. IT Equipment maintenance

K.7 DEFINITIONS OF COMMON ELEMENTS APPLICABLE TO STRATEGIC MISSILE SYSTEMS
ONLY

         Elements that are specifically unique to Strategic Missile Systems are defined in K.7. When the Strategic
Missile Systems Appendix is used the unique elements have been specifically grouped into four subcategories.
Lower level elements of these categories may be created but must sum to the correct category. The unique common
elements to be used are systems engineering, integration and test, program management, and support equipment
(SEIT/PM and Support Equipment). Also, Operational/Site Activation is also unique to the Strategic Missile
Systems.

          K.7.1 SEIT/PM and Support Equipment. This section provides the WBS elements common to all
Strategic Missile System WBS elements. Consistent with the manner in which strategic missile systems are
designed, built, and tested, there are multiple levels of Systems Engineering (SE), Integration and Test (AI&T),
and Program Management (PM) and Support Equipment throughout the WBS. It is expected that, whenever
possible, SEIT/PM and support equipment costs be reported with the item(s) they are supporting. For example,
subsystem management of the attitude control subsystem should be booked under the ACS WBS element. If a
contractor (or other developer) manages the project by a different means and does not collect any or all SEIT/PM
and Support Equipment elements at this level, then the costs for those elements shall be reported at the next
higher-level WBS element. For example, an electronics product manager with areas of responsibility spanning
both ACS and the Communications subsystems cost should have their effort defined and collected within the
AVE/FVE SEIT/PM element. SEIT/PM and Support Equipment shall be broken out into their individual elements
(i.e. Systems Engineering Assembly Integration and Test, Program Management, and Support Equipment each
defined below) for all WBS levels. At the lowest level, Systems Engineering and Program Management (SEPM)
responsibilities are often indistinct and may be combined into a single SEPM element.

                                                         225

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                  MIL-STD-881F
                                                  APPENDIX K


          K.7.1.1 Systems Engineering. This WBS element contains all the resources associated with all
engineering from functional specialists who provide technical planning, technical management, analysis, and
support efforts for development and production activities. The systems engineering entity is responsible for the
analysis, derivation, allocation, and traceability, of requirements, design, and interfaces. Systems engineering are
defined below.

         Systems Engineering includes, for example:
         a. Systems definition
         b. Systems analysis (e.g., thermal, power, mass properties, environments, dynamics, modeling and
             simulation)
         c. Requirements analysis and allocation
         d. Interface definition and control
         e. Performance assessment and verification/validation
         f. Technical direction (SE leadership, planning, and coordination)
         g. System safety
         h. Quality assurance, product assurance
         i. Mission assurance and critical skill protection and retention
         j. Logistics Support Analysis (LSA including reliability, availability, maintainability, producibility)
         k. Engineering services
         l. Configuration control
         m. System documentation
         n. Algorithm development
         o. Risk management
         p. Human engineering
         q. Security engineering
         r. Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC)/Electromagnetic Interference (EMI)
         s. Radiation and survivability
         t. Contamination and control
         u. Autonomy and fault management
         v. Other specialty engineering
         w. Engineering and design (excluding box level) including Electrical Design Integration (EDI),
             Mechanical Design Integration (MDI), Thermal Design Integration (TDI), and Facility Design
         x. Parts, materials, and processes (PM&P)
         y. Trade studies
         z. System studies

         K.7.1.1.1 Nuclear Hardening and Survivability (NH&S). This element is a quantitative description of
the physical attributes and capability of a system or component that will allow it to survive in a given nuclear
environment and continue to accomplish its mission. Nuclear hardness levels must be quantified and validated.

         K.7.1.1.2 System Safety. Other system engineering support including system requirement and
architecture definition, overall system design and margin management, design integrity analysis, system
optimization, system/cost effectiveness analysis, and intra-system and inter-system compatibility assurance, etc.;
safety engineering, human health, environmental protection, quality assurance program, value engineering. Also
includes Interface Management, Human Systems Integration, Supportability analyses, and Configuration
Management.

        K.7.1.1.3 Nuclear Surety. Efforts to assure nuclear weapons and their components do not become
vulnerable to loss, theft, sabotage, damage, or unauthorized use.

         K.7.1.2 Assembly, Integration and Test (AI&T). This element includes the effort of technical and
functional activities required to assemble and test at one level into a next higher level (e.g., from product level to
subsystem level) as a whole and not directly part of any other individual element.

         Includes, for example:

                                                         226

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                   MIL-STD-881F
                                                   APPENDIX K

          a. Development of test plans and procedures
          b. Test preparations
          c. Test support and management
          d. Hardware/software integration
          e. Software CSCI integration
          f. Hardware integration and assembly (e.g., electrical and mechanical integration, including kitting)
          g. Test, checkout, inspection and acceptance
          h. System test and evaluation, to include developmental test and evaluation, operational test and
             evaluation
          i. I&T management, leadership, planning and scheduling
          j. Analysis and documentation of test results
          k. Transportation and movement (excludes transportation of the space vehicle to the launch pad
             covered under the Launch Operations WBS element)
          l. Integration hardware
          m. Processing/handling of delivered initial spares and other repair parts
          n. I&T Facilities

           K.7.1.3 Program Management. Program Management (PM) includes the resources necessary to manage,
 direct, and control all effort contributing to the development, production of custom and commercial off-the-shelf
 (COTS) procurements and integration. Additionally, it includes training related items used to facilitate instruction
 through which personnel will learn to operate and maintain the system and elements of the system. Data related
 items are also captured here, such as the deliverable data required to be listed on a Contract Data Requirements
 List (CDRL).

          Includes, for example:
          a. Project management
          b. Business management (includes business operations., scheduling, accounting, and finance)
          c. Configuration management
          d. Contract/subcontract management
          e. Security management
          f. Delivered data and data summaries, including, for example:
               i. Technical publications and technical manuals
              ii. Cost and Schedule Reporting (IPMDAR, CFSR, IMS, WBS, etc.)
             iii. Training plans, manuals, guides
          g. Program control
          h. Data management including data repository and associated drafting and clerical effort to maintain
               master, Government approved, and Government owned documents
          i. Administration
          j. Product effectiveness
          k. Training services to include: course development, material preparation, and conducting initial
               training
          K.7.1.3.1 Program Control. Program/Project control and planning (i.e., business management, business
 operations, scheduling, accounting and finance), and cost, schedule, and performance measurement management.

          K.7.1.4 Support Equipment. This element pertains to the design, development, and production of those
items and associated software required to build, assemble, integrate, move, support, and maintain the system or
portions of the system. This also pertains to the testing and measurement equipment that allows an operator or
maintenance function to evaluate operational conditions of a system or equipment by performing specific
diagnostics, screening, or quality assurance efforts.

          Includes, for example:
          a. Peculiar support equipment (PSE)
          b. Common support equipment (CSE)
          c. Tooling, (e.g., taps, dies, etc.)
          d. Mock-ups/System Integration Labs (SILs), Simulators, Stimulators, and training equipment
          e. Vehicles, equipment, tools, and the like used to fuel, service, transport, hoist, repair, overhaul,

                                                          227

                             Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                           Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                  MIL-STD-881F
                                                  APPENDIX K

            assemble, disassemble, test, inspect, or otherwise maintain mission equipment
         f. Any production of duplicate or modified factory test or tooling equipment delivered to the
            Government for use in maintaining the system
         g. Support and handling equipment including ground, vehicular, powered, non-powered, and
            materiel handling
         h. Precision measuring equipment
         i. Instrumentation hardware
         j. Automatic test equipment
         k. Manual test equipment
         l. Test program sets
         m. Automated load modules
         n. Support equipment software

        K.7.2 Operational/Site Activation. The real estate, construction, conversion, utilities, and equipment to
provide all facilities required to house, service, and launch prime mission equipment at the organizational and
intermediate level.

          K.7.2.1 Deployment Planning. Outlining the scope, approach, and execution for the deployment of the
missile system in a deployment plan. The plan includes, where relevant, information about system operation and
support, escalation processes, roles and responsibilities before, during, and after deployment. The deployment plan
is intended to operators, stakeholders, and support personnel with a smooth transition of operational deployment.
The deployment plan describes each step of the deployment process at each deployment location, whether there is
one site or multiple sites, or one deployment or a phased deployment planned. The deployment plan defines all of
the work steps for complete deployment, and who does them.

         K.7.2.2 Site Construction. Real estate, site planning and preparation, construction, and other special-
purpose facilities necessary to achieve system operational status.

         K.7.2.3 Aerospace Ground Equipment. Support equipment that supplies electricity, hydraulic pressure,
air pressure, and other items required for missile maintenance and preparation for flight.

          K.7.2.4 Real Property Installed Equipment. Items of equipment that are affixed and built into the facility
as an integral part of the facility. Equipment that is an integral part of the facility, which if removed would destroy
or reduce the usefulness of the facility, heating, cooling, and electrical system and included in the cost of
construction and/renovation.

         K.7.2.5 Site/Ship/Vehicle Conversion. The materials and services required to convert existing sites, ships,
or vehicles to accommodate the mission equipment and selected support equipment directly related to the specific
system. Includes operations, support, and other special purpose facilities conversion necessary to achieve launch.

         K.7.2.6 System Assembly, Installation, and Checkout. All effort of technical and functional activities
associated with the design, development, and production of mating surfaces, structures, equipment, parts,
materials, and software required to assemble the Air Vehicle and Command & Launch equipment
(hardware/software) elements into the Missile System as a whole.

                                   APPENDIX L: SUSTAINMENT

 L.1 SCOPE

 This appendix provides the Work Breakdown Structure and Definitions related to the sustainment of systems
 (except for IS/DBS programs, use Appendix J “IS/DBS Sustainment structure” (J.5), which is tailored for IS/DBS
 programs in particular).

 L.2 APPLICABLE DOCUMENTS

          L.2.1 General. The documents listed in this section are specified in Appendix L of this Standard. This
 section does not include documents cited in other appendices of this Standard or recommended for additional
 information or as examples. While every effort has been made to ensure the completeness of this list, document
 users are cautioned that they must meet all specified requirements of documents cited in appendices of this
 Standard, whether or not they are listed.

         L.2.2   Government documents.

         L.2.2.1 Specifications, standards, and handbooks. The following specifications, standards, and handbooks
 form a part of this document to the extent specified herein. Unless otherwise specified, the issues of these
 documents are those cited in the solicitation or contract.

                                  DEFENSE ACQUISITION UNIVERSITY
                                Defense Acquisition Guidebook (Latest Edition)

(Copies of this document are available from https://www.dau.mil/tools/t/Defense-Acquisition-Guidebook or
Defense Acquisition University, 9820 Belvoir Rd, Fort Belvoir, VA 22060)

                       COST ASSESSMENT AND PROGRAM EVALUATION (CAPE)

Cost Assessment and Program Evaluation (CAPE) Operating and Support Cost Estimating Guide (Latest edition)

(Copies of this document are available from https://www.cape.osd.mil/files/OS_Guide_Sept_2020.pdf)

         L.2.2.2 Other Government documents, drawings, and publications. Not Applicable.

         L.2.3   Non-Government publications. Not Applicable.




                                                        229

                           Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                         Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                  MIL-STD-881F
                                                  APPENDIX L

L.3 IMPLEMENTATION OF SUSTAINMENT

          Sustainment often involves combinations of contractor and organic support which can vary widely from
program to program and is heavily influenced by acquisition pathway, sustainment strategy, system maturity,
investment into logistics support data, and organic sustainment requirements. The need to understand and collect
data in a standardized WBS for sustainment activities will often result in the need to utilize this sustainment WBS
even when a system is still in the acquisition phase of a program. Contractors are often responsible for initial
sustainment efforts during the acquisition phase of a program and therefore must report their activities for contract
purposes. Contracts which are primarily sustainment related (“Interim Contractor Support” or “Contractor
Logistics Support” for example) or include high cost, risk, or technical interest scope related to sustainment related
efforts should consider the use of the WBS included within Section L.4, even if the program is technically within
a development or acquisition phase. Contracts with both sustainment related efforts and non-sustainment related
efforts (development or production), should consider a combined product orientated appendix for non-sustainment
efforts and the WBS included within Section L.4 for sustainment related efforts. When determining if this
sustainment appendix, a product-orientated development and acquisition focused appendix, or a mixture of both
will be best to report a given contract or effort, the Program Manager should carefully consider:

          1) the purpose of a given action;
          2) how the execution and risks for the given action will be tracked;
          3) what data deliverables are being collected; and
          4) what information will be beneficial both to the current program and to other similar programs when at
          a comparable stage of their lifecycle.
         Regardless of the phase in which the work is performed, Government and contractor sustainment-centric
actions should be reported to provide consistency as support strategies change and mature.

L.5 DEFINITIONS

         L.5.1 Sustainment. Consists of efforts including operating, maintaining, and supporting a fielded system.
Specifically, this consists of (organic and contractor) personnel, equipment, supplies, software, and services
associated with operating, modifying, maintaining, supplying, and otherwise supporting a system in the DoD
inventory.

          Includes, for example:
          a. Interim Contractor Support (ICS) – ICS is temporary contractor support provided before a
              permanent support solution (organic or contractor) is established. This support consists of labor,
              material and overhead for maintenance, supply management, and other support functions during
              the early fielding of the system.
          b. Contractor Logistics Support (CLS) – If the permanent support solution is provided by a contractor,
              it is known as Contractor Logistics Support (CLS). CLS consists of labor, material, and overhead
              for maintenance, supply management, and other support functions required to sustain the system.
              The only difference between ICS and CLS is the period of performance. If CLS is used, it begins
              when the ICS period has ended.
          c. Performance Based Logistics (PBL) – PBL integrates the various product support activities with
              appropriate metrics and incentives, and product support requirements are described in terms of the
              desired results rather than in terms of how work is to be performed. The PBL strategy can apply to
              both CLS and organic maintenance.

          Excludes, for example:
          a. Indirect sustainment costs which may be considered and addressed separately in programming and
              budgeting. For additional information concerning O&S Indirect Costs, refer to the OSD CAPE
              O&S Cost-Estimating Guide Sept 2020, Appendix B.


         L.5.1.1 Unit-Level Manpower. This element includes all operator, maintenance, and other support
manpower at operating units (or at maintenance and support units that are organizationally related and adjacent to
the operating units).

          L.5.1.1.1 Operations. This element includes all manpower required to operate a system.

                                                         231

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                 MIL-STD-881F
                                                 APPENDIX L


                 Includes, for example:
                 a. Aircraft and Helicopters: Aircrews including pilots, navigators, mission specialists,
                     loadmasters, etc.
                 b. Ships: Command staff, combat information center personnel, fire control (if operations,
                     maintenance and other support categories are separate).
                 c. Electronic Systems: Console operators.
                 d. Armored Vehicles: tank commander, gunner, driver, loader.
                 e. Space Systems: Operators at the ground station or similar facility.

        L.5.1.1.2 Unit-Level Maintenance. This element includes all military, civilian, and contractor manpower
performing field-level maintenance on the system. This element includes the organizational maintenance
manpower (usually resident in the system operating unit) and unit-level intermediate maintenance personnel. For
the Navy and Air Force, this element includes organizational maintenance manpower and unit-level intermediate
maintenance personnel.

         Excludes, for example:
         a. The intermediate-level maintenance personnel resident in a support organization that is not unit-
             level relative to the operating unit, such as a Navy shore-based Intermediate Maintenance Activity.
             Such intermediate-level maintenance personnel are included in L.5.1.3.3 (Intermediate
             Maintenance (External to Unit-Level)).

         L.5.1.1.3 Other Unit-Level (1…n Specify). This element includes military, civilian, and contractor
manpower performing administrative, security, logistics, safety, engineering, field support, and other mission
support functions at the unit-level. These include only manpower positions that exist to support the system wholly
or predominately support the system under consideration. For systems that deploy, these include manpower
positions that routinely deploy to support the system.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Staff. Manpower required for unit command, administration, supervision, operations control,
             planning, scheduling, safety, quality control of crew training and operational proficiency, etc. This
             may also include staff in a parent organization above the unit level where appropriate (i.e., staff
             is primarily dedicated to the system).
         b. Security. Manpower required for system security. Duties may include system level entry control,
             close and distant boundary support, and security alert operations. Also includes base access
             control but only when the entire facility exists solely to support the weapon system.
         c. Logistics. Manpower required for logistics support. Functions may include supply, transportation,
             inventory control, fuel handling, etc.
         d. Ordnance Support. Includes manpower providing munitions handling, weapons assembly, etc.
         e. Other Support. Manpower required to provide system-specific fixed and mobile communications,
             information, intelligence, photo interpretation, and other special mission support.

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Base level access control when the facility exists to support multiple weapon system.
         b. Manpower associated with operations or maintenance of simulators or training devices captured in
             L.5.1.4.6 (Simulator Operations and Repair).
         c. Excludes any ordnance support manpower included in L.5.1.1.2 (Unit-Level Maintenance).

         L.5.1.2 Unit Operations. This element includes operating material and various support services in support
of the system at the unit level.

        L.5.1.2.1 Energy. (Fuel, Petroleum, Oil and Electricity). Includes fuel, petroleum, oil, and fuel additives
used by systems in performing their normal missions. These also include field-generated electricity and
commercial electricity necessary to support the operation of a system, when it can be severed from the general
energy consumption for the location.


                                                        232

                           Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                         Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                 MIL-STD-881F
                                                 APPENDIX L

         L.5.1.2.2 Training Munitions and Expendable Stores. This element includes unit-level consumption of
live and inert training munitions, rockets, missiles, ammunition, bombs, rockets, sonobuoys, pyrotechnics, and
expendable stores such as chaff, flares, fuel tanks, travel pods or other items that lose their identity in use.

        L.5.1.2.3 Support Services. This element includes services attributable to unit-level system support not
otherwise accounted for.

         Includes, for example:
         a. FSRs for non-maintenance activities such as training, data collection, and IT support.
         b. Un-reimbursed food services, rations, postal services (postage/box rental), and laundry services.
         c. Lease, rental, or service contracts for administrative, computational, or support equipment or
             software.
         d. Lease of special facilities or land (e.g., for storage of warheads and missiles).
         e. Communications services (e.g., data/voice links, dedicated lines, microwave channels), port services,
             and other unit-funded utilities not part of base operating support.

          L.5.1.2.4 Temporary Duty (TDY) Travel. This element includes a military service member's or
Department of Defense civilian employee's travel or other assignment at a location other than the traveler's
permanent duty station as authorized by the Joint Travel Regulations. This type of assignment is usually of
relatively short duration, typically from two days to 189 days in length. This element includes per diem pay,
covering lodging, meals, and incidental expenses.

         L.5.1.2.5 Second Destination Transportation. This element includes transportation in support of the unit.
Typically includes transportation for moving equipment, personnel, and supplies to and from training areas, remote
operating sites, or test ranges. Excludes any transportation costs included in overhead in other WBS elements, such
as the overhead in the depot maintenance element for the transportation of systems for depot maintenance
overhauls.

          L.5.1.3 Maintenance. This element includes labor (outside of the scope of unit-level) and materials at all
levels of maintenance in support of the system.

         L.5.1.3.1 Consumables. This element includes parts used to operate and maintain the system at the unit
level. Typically, consumables are considered throw-away items (having a 100% condemnation rate) and are
replaced by new items. Examples include temperature sensors, fans, oil filters, valves, switches, probes, coolants,
deicing fluids, deicing fluids, gaskets, capacitors, and batteries. This element also includes any initial
transportation, storage, warehousing, inventory management, and other overheads incurred in order to procure and
stock consumable parts.

          L.5.1.3.2 Depot Level Reparables (DLRs). This element includes items/components that are repaired at
the depot level of maintenance. Typically, DLRs are removed from the system at the unit-level and then sent to a
facility to perform the depot level maintenance. DLRs are inspected by the depot level maintenance activity and
may be subsequently repaired and put back into an inventory management system or may be condemned after
being deemed uneconomical to repair. This element also includes any parts transportation, storage, warehousing,
inventory management, and other overheads incurred in order to procure, stock, repair, or condemn DLR parts. If
a credit is provided when a “failed” item/component is turned in for depot level maintenance, that credit is also
included within this element.

         L.5.1.3.3 Intermediate Maintenance (External to Unit-Level). This element includes labor, material, and
any other efforts expended at intermediate maintenance locations (such as Navy afloat or ashore Intermediate
Maintenance Activities) in support of the system. This element includes consumables, Government labor,
contractor maintenance, and any other intermediate-level maintenance not otherwise accounted for.

         L.5.1.3.4 Depot Maintenance. This element includes labor, material, and overhead efforts associated with
major overhauls or other similar depot-level maintenance on a system or any of its major end items (e.g. aircraft
engines) at centralized repair depots, contractor repair facilities, or onsite by depot teams. This element includes
overhauls which are periodic, frequently scheduled, major maintenance events whereby a system undergoes

                                                        233

                           Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                         Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                   MIL-STD-881F
                                                   APPENDIX L

 general maintenance and alterations at a depot-level repair activity.

           L.5.1.3.4.1 Scheduled Depot Maintenance. This element includes scheduled maintenance at the depot
 level.

          L.5.1.3.4.2 Unscheduled Depot Maintenance. This element includes unscheduled maintenance at the
 depot level.
           L.5.1.4 Sustaining Support. This element includes support services provided by centrally managed
 support activities external to the units that own the operating system. Effort associated with this element must be
 attributable to a specific system and exclude effort that would be arbitrarily allocated.
            L.5.1.4.1 System Specific Training. This element includes system-specific specialty training of
 individuals in the system operating units or individuals who are replacing people in the system operating units due
 to attrition and normal rotation. This element includes the instructors, training support personnel, per diem and
 travel directly associated with the training, and any trainee effort not accounted for elsewhere.

          L.5.1.4.1.1 System-Specific Operator Training. This element includes training at primary training sites
 for personnel to become proficient in specific system knowledge. It includes units such as Air Force wings assigned
 a primary mission of weapon-specific aircrew training, Navy air readiness training units, Navy Afloat Training
 Groups, and the Army Armor Center.

          Excludes, for example:
          a. Providing skill training unrelated to a specific system, e.g., undergraduate aviation training.
          L.5.1.4.1.2 System-Specific Maintenance Training. This element includes providing advanced system-
specific training associated with maintenance in units designated as primary training facilities.

         L.5.1.4.2 Support Equipment Replacement and Repair. This element includes replacement or repair unit-
 level support equipment associated with the system or its major subsystems. The support equipment (e.g., tools
 and test sets) may be peculiar to the system or may be common with other systems.

           Excludes, for example:
           a. Initial support equipment procurement, which is an investment, not an O&S related effort.

         L.5.1.4.3 Sustaining/Systems Engineering. This element includes Government and contractor sustaining
 engineering to ensure the continuing viable operation of the system in the deployed environment. Most of the
 sustaining engineering effort will be a continuation of the earlier systems engineering effort that took place during
 development or production and is included here when not directly applicable to other WBS.

           Includes, for example:
           a. Aircraft structural integrity monitoring or corrosion monitoring
           b. Planning and control of technical program efforts
           c. Continuing system requirements definition
           d. Safety and human systems integration engineering
           e. Obsolescence engineering
           f. Configuration management
           g. Continuing specialty engineering, such as Reliability and Maintainability (R&M) Engineering
           h. Continuing Integrated Logistics Support

           Excludes, for example:
           a. Specific modifications to hardware or software (which are included in element L.5.1.5 Continuing
               System Improvements).
          L.5.1.4.4 Program Management. This element includes Government and contractor effort for management
activities associated with the administrative, business, and financial management of the program. Program
management activities are similar to those performed during development or production and is included here when

                                                          234

                             Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                           Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                  MIL-STD-881F
                                                  APPENDIX L

not directly applicable to other WBS.
         L.5.1.4.5 Data and Technical Publications. This element includes maintaining and updating deliverable
data and technical publications and manuals concerning the operation and support of the system.

          Excludes, for example:
          b. Initial data and publication development and procurement, which is an investment, not an O&S
              related effort.

        L.5.1.4.6 Simulator Operations and Repair. This element includes effort related to operating and repairing
simulators and other training devices for the system or its major subsystems. It may include labor, material and
overhead for simulator operations. Also includes periodic replacement of simulator hardware and software.

         L.5.1.4.6.1 Simulator/Training Device Operations. This element includes effort associated with simulator
and training device operations.

        L.5.1.4.6.2 Simulator/Training Device Hardware Repair. This element includes effort associated with
simulator and training device hardware repair and replace activities.

        L.5.1.4.6.3 Simulator/Training Device Software Support. This element includes effort associated with
simulator and training device software support and refresh activities.
         L.5.1.4.7 Other Sustaining Support 1…n (Specify). This element includes any significant sustaining
support effort not otherwise accounted for. This element may be used to identify expenses such as those listed
below, if they apply to the system under consideration.

        Includes, for example:
        a. Test and evaluation in support of deployed systems, such as range costs, test support, data reduction,
            and test reporting when testing is confirming that the system continues to retain its operational
            capabilities.
        b. Air, sea, and land support not funded by the unit and provided by other activities to verify the proper
            operation of an electronic, communication, sensor, or other similar system.
        c. Communication services (e.g., data/voice links, dedicated lines, microwave channels), hardware, and
            software leases purchased on a DoD-wide or Service-wide basis for direct system specific support of
            a system.
        d. Centrally funded purchases for transportation of system materiel (end items and secondary items) not
            otherwise accounted for.

         Excludes, for example:
        a. Test and evaluation in support of deployed systems, such as range costs, test support, data reduction,
            and test reporting when testing supports development activities or testing integral to system hardware
            modification or software maintenance (which are applicable to element L.5.1.5 Continuing System
            Improvements).
        b. Communication services purchased at the unit-level are contained in WBS L.5.1.2.3 (Support
            Services).

          L.5.1.5 Continuing System Improvements. This element includes the hardware and software updates that
 occur after deployment of a system. These updates improve a system's safety, reliability, maintainability, or
 performance characteristics and enable the system to meet its basic operational requirements throughout its life.

          Excludes, for example:
          a. System improvements identified as part of an incremental evolutionary acquisition strategy or pre-
              planned product improvement program as these are not considered part of the O&S phase.

         L.5.1.5.1 Hardware Modifications. This element includes development, procurement, and installation of
 modification kits on the system, support equipment, and training devices. This element also includes updates to


                                                         235

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                  MIL-STD-881F
                                                  APPENDIX L

 technical publications/data, and initial spares and repair parts when updates are consistent with the approved
 modification content.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Modifications needed to achieve acceptable safety levels, overcome mission capability deficiencies,
             improve reliability, reduce maintenance costs, or maintain the system service life.

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Modifications undertaken to provide additional operational capability not called for in the original
             system design or performance specifications, or extend the system service life beyond what was
             originally planned.
         b. Retrofit modifications that update earlier fielded systems to the latest production configuration.

        L.5.1.5.1.1 Mod Kit Development. This element includes the design and development of subsystem,
 component, and parts changes.

          L.5.1.5.1.2 Mod Kit Procurement. This element includes the procurement of subsystem, component, and
 parts changes.

        L.5.1.5.1.3 Mod Kit Initial Spares. This element includes the initial spares associated with the subsystem,
 component, and parts changes.

          L.5.1.5.1.4 Mod Kit Installation. This element includes the installation of subsystem, component, and
 parts changes.

         L.5.1.5.2 Software Maintenance. This element includes all efforts related to the maintenance of previously
released software used within a system.

         L.5.1.5.2.1 Software Changes. This element captures all effort to alter, sustain, and support the system
software baseline.

         Includes, for example:
          a. Correction of deficiencies, defects, and cybersecurity.
          b. Refactoring (restructuring the existing body of code without altering behavior).
          c. Minor enhancements.
          d. Independent Verification and Validation (IV&V) review secured through a separate contract with a
              third party that ensures that the system is well engineered and meets agreed upon requirements
              (verification) and that the system meets the users’ needs (validation).
          e. Changes for adaptive, corrective, perfective, additive, and preventive maintenance.

         Excludes, for example:
         c. Development of new requirements that are major enhancements or significant increases in capability.

         L.5.1.5.2.2 Software Program Management. This element includes all management of software, including
the implementation, delivery, and sustainment of software and is included here when not directly applicable to other
WBS.

         L.5.1.5.2.3 Software Sustaining Engineering. This element includes all software-specific sustaining
engineering and is included here when not directly applicable to other WBS.

         L.5.1.5.2.4 System Facilities. This element includes system-specific effort associated with establishing and
operating an environment such as a Software Integration Laboratory (SIL) for software maintenance-related
development and testing.

        L.5.1.5.2.5 Field Service Engineers. This element includes the effort associated with on-site support of a
deployed software product or system in its operational environment and is included here when not directly

                                                         236

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                   MIL-STD-881F
                                                   APPENDIX L

applicable to other WBS.

         L.5.1.5.2.6 Certification and Accreditation. This element includes activities such as Cybersecurity,
Information Assurance Vulnerability Management (IAVM), Airworthiness, Safety, and Networthiness.
Cybersecurity, formerly Information Assurance (IA), and the Risk Management Framework (RMF) for DoD
Information Technology, formerly DoD Information Assurance Certification and Accreditation Process (DIACAP),
are processes that verify the software system against externally defined domain performance criteria.

        L.5.1.5.2.7 Help Desk. This element captures effort required to provide help desk support for end users.
May include effort associated with answering and filtering help desk calls, troubleshooting, account management,
system administration, software upgrades, and break/fix.

        Includes, for example:
        a. Tier 1 support provided by a central call center for basic customer issues such as solving usage
            problems and fulfilling service desk requests that need IT involvement.
        b. Tier 2 support provided by experienced and knowledgeable support personnel who assess issues and
            provide solutions for problems.
        c. Tier 3 support provided by highly skilled product specialists who have an expert knowledge of the
            application and can resolve issues or problems and fix defects in the production baseline by changing
            the system software.

         L.5.1.5.2.8 Hosting. This element includes software hosting, either in a traditional data center or in the
cloud. Includes activities associated with computing resources and servers that execute all or some portion of an
application from a central location across a network. Also includes storage and services necessary to maintain and
manage server hardware and operations. If system is cloud hosting, consider if additional specificity for
infrastructure as a service, vs. platform as a service, vs. software as a service should be collected.

           L.5.1.5.2.9 Software Licenses. This element includes license procurement and license maintenance fees
for all licenses necessary to operate and maintain the system. This includes licenses in the development environment
as well as the deployed software system.

                                                  APPENDIX M

         NAPPENDIX M: GOVERNMENT SYSTEM TEST AND EVALUATION
                        COMMODITY DEFINITIONS

M.1 SCOPE

         This appendix provides the definitions for Government System Test and Evaluation (ST&E) WBS
elements, which are unique to the Defense Materiel Items defined in Appendices A-J. All elements are specific to
ST&E efforts. The purpose for these definitions is to support the Government in identifying, collecting, and
reporting costs associated with Government ST&E actions and activities. It is for this reason this appendix has
been developed.

M.2 APPLICABLE DOCUMENTS
         M.2.1 General. The documents listed in this section are specified in Appendix M of this Standard. This
section does not include documents cited in other appendices of this Standard or recommended for additional
information or as examples. While every effort has been made to ensure the completeness of this list, document
users are cautioned that they must meet all specified requirements of documents cited in appendices of this
Standard, whether or not they are listed.

         M.2.2 Government documents.

         M.2.2.1 Specifications, standards, and handbooks. The following specifications, standards, and
handbooks form a part of this document to the extent specified herein. Unless otherwise specified, the issues of
these documents are those cited in the solicitation or contract.

                                             Managers”, December 31 2020

                              Army Regulation 73-1 Test and Evaluation Policy June 8, 2018

         M.2.3 Non-Government publications. The following documents form a part of this document to the extent
specified herein. Unless otherwise specified, the issues of these documents are those cited in the solicitation or contract.

          M.3 Key Principles in Constructing a WBS. In this appendix of the MIL-STD, the WBS is defined to

                                                         238

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                   MIL-STD-881F
                                                   APPENDIX M

 Level 3, 4, or 5 depending on the system. WBS elements in the appendices may be extended to appropriate
 levels below their MIL-STD definition for informational or reporting purposes. The purpose for these
 extensions is to ensure that the higher-level elements include the proper lower level elements when required to
 report at a lower level. If an extension below the MIL-STD definition is required, maintaining the product-
 oriented decomposition is required.

           1) Extending the WBS to Lower Levels. The reporting level of the WBS depends on the level of
 interest where work is accomplished. Reporting at levels below those identified within the MIL-STD should
 be reported if they are considered high cost, high risk, high technical, and/or special interest. For those
 elements, extension of the WBS to lower levels may be necessary to get needed visibility, but only for those
 elements. Not all WBS elements should be extended to the lowest level. In addition, for each system being
 defined only those WBS elements that define the system shall be used. An example of the extension for this
 appendix commodity can be found at https://cade.osd.mil/policy/csdr-plan.

           2) 100% Rule. A key to WBS development is the principle that if the effort and resources can be
 associated with the element they support, they should be included within that element. This is called the 100%
 rule, which states the next level of decomposition of a WBS element (child level) must represent 100% of the
 work applicable to the next higher level (parent level). For example, the parent level WBS (Fire Control) has
 three child elements—transmitter, antenna, and receiver. If the program manager decides he/she wants more
 visibility into the transmitter subsystem and pulls it out of the Fire Control system to make it a level equal to
 the Fire Control, it distorts the effort and resources that are required to complete that Fire Control system. The
 reason is that the transmitter has lost its parent (Fire Control)/child relationship because it assumes the
 transmitter is not included within the Fire Control system.

           3) Identifying Parent/Child Relationship. In some cases, items cannot be specifically associated with
 the element they support. For example, software is a critical element of that transmitter within the Fire Control.
 Under normal circumstances, software would be the child level to the parent level transmitter. However,
 depending on how software is developed, it may include more functionality than just the transmitter subsystem.
 It may, for instance, include functionality for the receiver as well. In this case the software cannot be associated
 with the specific elements they support, due to the inability to determine the effort for each functionality
 developed. It is, therefore, appropriate to associate that software to the next higher level (Fire Control) of the
 WBS. To accomplish this, it will be included in the Fire Control Software element of the Fire Control system.
 This eliminates allocation of the effort across multiple WBS elements where it is difficult to determine what
 level of support each gets.

            4) Recognizing Cybersecurity Processes/Products. Cybersecurity is a requirement for all DoD
 programs and must be fully considered and implemented in all aspects of acquisition programs across the life
 cycle. Attention must be paid to cybersecurity at all acquisition category levels and all classification levels,
 including unclassified, throughout the entire life cycle; this includes systems that reside on networks and stand-
 alone systems that are not persistently connected to networks during tactical and strategic operations. Since
 responsibility for cybersecurity extends to every member of the acquisition workforce, DoD and its contractors
 need to design, develop, and acquire systems that can operate in applicable cyber threat environments. As such
 it is critical to understand what the cost of these systems is (i.e., hardware, software, systems engineering,
 program management, and system test and evaluation). MIL-STD-881F supports this effort by providing the
 structure to identify, collect and report many of these critical costs. In this appendix, we are focused on System
 Test and Evaluation efforts. It is also recognized gathering this information is difficult at the level of detail
 these systems are developed. It is for this reason, where cybersecurity related costs can be easily accounted for,
 they should be associated with the WBS they support. For example, for hardware/software elements, if the test
 and evaluation effort for the subsystem is specifically a cybersecurity activity, then call out the test that supports
 the effort of that WBS element. If the test supports System Test and Evaluation efforts, the test is performed at
 the system level and should be associated with the System Test and Evaluation WBS (i.e., DT&E, OT&E).

         5) Numbering of the WBS. In each appendix, the work breakdown structure for that commodity has
been numbered for reporting purposes only. In addition, the numbering provides visible clarity regarding level
of indenture and parent-child content. While the numbering system is numeric, several unique issues arise


                                                         239

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                   MIL-STD-881F
                                                   APPENDIX M

 across appendices which require the numbering system to be modified to accommodate these anomalies (See
 Figure XI). Additionally, if a WBS element is not required, it may be eliminated from the structure and the
 numbering would be logically adjusted.

           6) Other 1...n (Specify) WBS Element Definitions. All of the individual appendices in Appendix M
 contain a WBS element titled “Other”1…n (Specify) at the subsystem, element (product) levels that are restricted
 for tests that have not been envisioned or predicted within the defined WBS elements of the commodity. This is
 available to provide flexibility within the system WBS for new or additional WBS elements that are not identified
 or defined in the Standard. These “other” elements would be used if, for example, a new cyber test or modified
 cyber subsystems test is needed, but it does not currently appear in Appendix M of MIL-STD-881F or the test has
 not been specifically identified in the standard. If it is determined that the “other” WBS is not needed, this element
 should not be used in the WBS. If it is determined that the “other” element is needed, then each element must be
 defined and the word “other” replaced by the newly defined WBS element (test name or type, etc.). As many as
 necessary may be defined using this format.

           7) Accounting for the Cost of ST&E. Determining the cost of system test and evaluation follows the
 100% rule which is described in 2) and 3) above. For example, performing a test may include many different
 members of industry or Government to support these efforts. These personnel may not directly report to the test
 community but are there to provide their expertise in support of the testing efforts by using other disciplines (i.e.,
 System Engineering, Training, PMO, etc.). Their efforts would be, by definition of the 100% rule, associated with
 ST&E activity they are supporting and not to the community to which they directly report. By doing so, they ensure
 the parent-child relationship stays whole. In other words, if System Engineering personnel are supporting the
 testing of a subsystem for a specific commodity, their efforts would be associated with that testing effort and not
 charged to the System Engineering organization, thereby maintaining the 100% rule and the parent-child
 relationship. Similarly, for the use of pilot, prototype, production, or specifically fabricated hardware/software to
 obtain or validate engineering/test data on the performance of the system in the developmental phase, would follow
 the same rules. The cost of building those assets specifically for testing purposes only, are associated with (charged
 to) the testing effort. If a specific Government program is being tested at a Major Range and Test Facility Base
 installation, the direct costs of using that facility in support of that program is paid by that program and is not a
 facility cost. The cost for maintaining and modernizing Government test facilities available for use by all users is
 typically separately funded as an institutional cost, not chargeable to any individual PMO.

       M.3.1 This appendix (M) provides Government System Test and Evaluation (ST&E) definitions for each
 commodity identified in this Standard. This includes:
          a.   Aircraft Systems
          b.   Electronic/Avionics/Generic Systems
          c.   Missile/Ordnance Systems
          d.   Strategic Missile Systems
          e.   Sea Systems
          f.   Space Systems
          g.   Ground Systems
          h.   Unmanned Maritime Systems
          i.   Launch Vehicle Systems
          j.   Information Systems/Defense Business Systems.
         The key elements of the Government System Test and Evaluation efforts are Developmental Test and
Evaluation (DT&E), Operational Test and Evaluation (OT&E) and Live Fire Test and Evaluation (LFT&E). For the
purpose of identifying the WBS for Government ST&E in each commodity, we have highlighted the ST&E portion
of the WBS within each commodity.

         M.3.2 Integrated Testing. The defense acquisition process is encouraging the use of Integrated Test. Integrated
Test methods can provide early discovery of system problems. DoDI 5000.89 defines Integrated Testing as the
collaborative planning and collaborative execution of test phases and events to provide shared data in support of
independent analysis, assessment, evaluation, and reporting by all stakeholders, particularly developmental (both
contractor and Government), and operational T&E communities. The integrated testing approach is documented in the


                                                          240

                             Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                           Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                  MIL-STD-881F
                                                  APPENDIX M

program TEMP. The data pedigree (test conditions and methodologies) are coordinated with the stakeholders prior to
execution of the test event.

         Integrated testing serves as an implementation concept for test design, not as a new type of T&E. Programs
intentionally design integrated testing into the earliest program strategies, plans, documentation, and test plans,
preferably starting before Milestone A. Developing and adopting integrated testing strategies early in the process
increases the opportunities and benefits. When done correctly, integrated testing provides greater opportunity for
early identification of system design improvements and may even change the course of system development during
EMD. Integrated testing is generally more appropriate once the system design has stabilized and the concept of
operations is understood. Integrated testing may reduce the scope and number of T&E resources needed in OT&E,
if no deficiencies are uncovered and no further design changes are made. However, integrated testing does not
replace or eliminate the need for dedicated Initial Operational Test & Evaluation (IOT&E), as required by 10 USC
2399 and DoDI 5000.89

         M.3.3 Applying the Integrated Test Work Breakdown Structure (WBS). The WBS defined for each
commodity in this appendix does not apply an Integrated Test approach. This appendix uses the traditional (separate
DT&E, OT&E and LFT&E) approach. It is recognized that the use of Integrated Testing is becoming more
prevalent, therefore, a new WBS which captures the integrated test approach would be required. If a program is not
applying an integrated test approach, the Government System Test and Evaluation WBS identified for each
commodity in the following sections of Appendix M, should be applied.

         M.4.1 System Test and Evaluation. The use of pilot, prototype, production, or specifically
fabricated hardware/software to obtain or validate engineering data on the performance of the system during
the developmental phase of the program. It also includes all effort associated with the development of any
specialized tools or data in support of the system level test program.

          Includes, for example:
          a. Detailed planning, conduct, support, data reduction and reports (excluding the contract data
              requirements list data) from such testing, and test articles that are functionally configured to represent
              and test subsystems/components of the defense materiel end item being developed or produced
          b. Design and production of models, specimens, fixtures and instrumentation

                                                         242

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                  MIL-STD-881F
                                                  APPENDIX M

         c.   Development and/or validation of modeling and simulation resources required for Test and
              Evaluation of a specific system under test.
         d.   System level labs that integrate both hardware and software
         e.   Training for Government personnel to provide knowledge and capability to deliver test and
              evaluation support actions as required.


NOTE: Test articles that are functionally configured to represent and test the complete defense
materiel end item being developed or produced, are excluded from this WBS element.

         Excludes, for example:
         a. All formal and informal testing up through the subsystem level, which can be associated with the
             hardware/software element acceptance testing

NOTE: These excluded efforts are to be included with the appropriate hardware or software elements.


          M.4.1.1 Developmental Test and Evaluation. (DT&E) is conducted throughout the acquisition process to
assist in engineering design and development and to verify that technical performance specifications have been
met. DT&E is planned and monitored by the developing agency and is normally conducted by the contractor. It
includes the T&E of components, subsystems, Preplanned Product Improvement (P3I) changes, hardware/software
integration, and production qualification testing. It encompasses the use of models, simulations, test beds, and
prototypes or full-scale engineering development models of the system.

         Includes test and evaluation conducted to:
         a. Demonstrate that the engineering design and development process is complete
         b. Demonstrate that the design risks have been minimized
         c. Demonstrate that the system will meet specifications
         d. Assess the system’s military utility when introduced
         e. Determine whether the engineering design is supportable (practical, maintainable, safe, etc.) for
              operational use
         f. Provide test data with which to examine and evaluate trade-offs against specification requirements,
              life cycle cost, and schedule
         g. Perform the logistics testing efforts to evaluate the achievement of supportability goals, the
              adequacy of the support package for the system, (e.g., deliverable maintenance tools, test
              equipment, technical publications, maintenance instructions, and personnel skills and training
              requirements, etc.)

         M.4.1.1.1 System Acceptance Test. To verify in an operational environment (either simulated or actual
production) that the complete system (i.e., the full complement of application software running on the target
hardware and systems software infrastructure) satisfies specified requirements (e.g., functional, performance, and
security) and is acceptable to end users.

         M.4.1.1.2 Wind Tunnel Tests. Tests producing the effects of movement through air or resistance to
moving air on models of aircraft. Provided that the airstream is properly controlled, it is immaterial whether the
stationary model under testing is designed to move through the air, as an aircraft, or to withstand wind pressures
while standing in place.

       M.4.1.1.3 Structural Tests. Static and fatigue testing for full-scale aircraft structures and major
components to receive airworthiness certification.

         M.4.1.1.4 Flight Tests. Provide data to substantiate design assumptions or demonstrate aeronautical
engineering during flight of an aircraft and then analyzes the data to evaluate the aerodynamic flight characteristics
of the vehicle in order to validate the design, including safety aspects.



                                                         243

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                  MIL-STD-881F
                                                  APPENDIX M

          M.4.1.1.5 Ground Tests. Ground testing of multiple aspects: basic aircraft vehicle operation, flight
controls, engine performance, dynamic systems stability evaluation, structural loads, etc.

          M.4.1.1.6 Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation. The developmental test and evaluation (DT&E) conducted
and monitored by the developing agency of the DoD. The testing activities will be focused on Test and Analyses.
This will determine the DT&E results for Low Rate Initial Production and the Limited Deployment Phase associated
with Cooperative Vulnerability Identification and Adversarial Cybersecurity. See Figure XII for Cybersecurity
Testing by phase requirements. Such activities include, for example, developing cybersecurity test objectives, testing
security standards, operational resilience, and system cyber survivability. Also includes planning cybersecurity test
infrastructure, adversarial DT&E, and test operational resistance. The following DoD Guidebooks and Instruction
(latest versions) should be followed for Cybersecurity test and evaluation planning; in addition to specific Service-
related guidebooks or instructions, as appropriate.

         a.   DoD Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation Guidebook
         b.   Army Regulation 73-1 Test and Evaluation Policy
         c.   Director, Operational Test and Evaluation (DOT&E), Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP)
              Guidebook,
         d.   DoD Instruction, No. 8510.01; “Risk Management Framework (RMF) for DoD Information
              Technology”
         e.   DoD Instruction 5000.83 “Technology and Program Protection to Maintain Technological
              Advantage”
         f.   DoD Instruction 5000.90 “Cybersecurity and Acquisition Decision Authorities and Program
              Managers”

         M.4.1.1.7 Service/Agency DT&E 1...n (Specify). The costs to rent, lease, procure, or utilize a military
weapon system in order to support the testing of a different, primary weapon system. These costs are normally
paid to by the primary weapon system program being tested to another program office/program in order to utilize
equipment or systems needed to test or characterize the primary weapon system.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Costs to utilize a ship, aircraft, ground vehicle used to act as an opposing force (e.g., “red team”).
         b. Costs to repair or demilitarize a non-primary military system damaged during the testing of the
             primary weapon system.

         Excludes, for example:
         b. Costs to procure, repair, or demilitarize the primary weapon system being tested.

         M.4.1.1.8 Other DT&E Tests 1…n (Specify). Other DT&E tests not captured elsewhere in this
commodity. The following are examples of DT&E tests that may be applicable to this commodity but were
not identified. If pertinent, include those that may be appropriate, by specifically listing them by starting with
this WBS element (M.4.1.8) NOTE: Each test should be identified by the next consecutive WBS number (e.g.,
M.4.1.8.1, M.4.1.8.2, etc.).
         Examples of Developmental Tests
         a. Market Research effort (or proof of concept test) and Evaluation
         b. Technical Feasibility Test (TFT) and Evaluation
         c. Engineering development test (EDT) and Evaluation
         d. Production prove-out test (PPT) and Evaluation
         e. Production qualification Test (PQT) and Evaluation
         f. Software development test (SDT) and Evaluation
         g. Software qualification test (SQT) and Evaluation
         h. Interoperability certification test and Evaluation
         i. Soldier protection testing and Evaluation
         j. Technical Manual (TM) Verification
         k. Physical configuration audit (PCA)/Functional configuration audit (FCA)
