         Examples of Pre-Operational Tests
         a. Production Verification Test (PVT) and Evaluation
         b. First Article Test (FAT) and Evaluation
         c. Comparison test
         d. Quality Conformance Inspection
         e. Lot acceptance test
         f. Post-Deployment Software Support (PDSS) Test
         g. Surveillance Test
         h. Reconditioning Test (Pilot or Initial)
         i. Control Test
         j. Acceptance Test
         k. Baseline Evaluation Test
         l. Regression Test (unique to software-intensive systems)
          M.4.1.2 Operational Test and Evaluation. The test and evaluation conducted by agencies other than
the developing command to assess the prospective system’s military utility, operational effectiveness,
operational suitability, logistics supportability (including compatibility, inter-operability, reliability,
maintainability, logistic requirements, etc.) The system is also being tested against the operational doctrine
suitable for the system against the threats expected to be encountered by the system, cost of ownership, and need
for any modifications.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Initial operational test and evaluation conducted during the development of a system
         b. Such tests as system demonstration, flight tests, sea trials, mobility demonstrations, on-orbit tests,
             spin demonstration, stability tests, qualification operational test and evaluation, etc., and support
             thereto, required to prove the operational capability of the deliverable system.
         c. Contractor support (e.g., technical assistance, maintenance, labor, material, etc.) consumed during
             this phase of testing
         d. Logistics testing efforts to evaluate the achievement of supportability goals and the adequacy of
             the support for the system (e.g., deliverable maintenance tools, test equipment, technical
             publications, maintenance instructions, personnel skills and training requirements, and software
             support facility/environment elements).
         e. Pre-full-rate production/full deployment tests - Common to all systems, including (1) Early User Test
             (EUT). (2) Limited User Test (LUT). (3) Initial Operating Test (IOT).
         f. Post-full-rate production/full deployment tests, including: (1) Full Operational Test (FOT). (2) User
             acceptance test (UAT) and unique to IT systems test.
         g. As-required tests, including (1) Customer test (CT) (common to all systems). (2) Supplemental site
             test (SST) (unique to IT systems). (3) Regression test (unique to software-intensive systems).

         M.4.1.2.1 Limited User Evaluation (LUE/M-LUT/FUE). Any type of RDTE funded user test conducted
that does not address all the effectiveness, suitability, and survivability issues and is therefore limited in comparison
to an IOT that must address all effectiveness, suitability, and survivability issues. The Limited User Test (LUT)
addresses a limited number of operational issues.
         M.4.1.2.2 Interoperability Testing (IOP with IA/OT&E). The process of assessing the ability of a system
to exchange usable electronic information with systems of other services or nations as specified in its requirements
documents.

         M.4.1.2.3 Flight Tests. Provide data to substantiate design assumptions or demonstrate aeronautical
engineering during flight of an aircraft and then analyzes the data to evaluate the aerodynamic flight characteristics
of the vehicle in order to validate the design, including safety aspects.

          M.4.1.2.4 Ground Tests. Ground testing of multiple aspects: basic aircraft vehicle operation, flight
controls, engine performance, dynamic systems stability evaluation, structural loads, etc.
          M.4.1.2.5 Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation. The operational test and evaluation (OT&E) conducted and
monitored by DoD agencies. The technical efforts of ensuring the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                   MIL-STD-881F
                                                   APPENDIX M

information and technology by monitoring and defending against cyber (network) attacks. Early operational testing
(that is, EUT and LUT), as well as Initial Operation and Test (IOT) will determine the OT&E results for Vulnerability
and Penetration Assessment and Adversarial Assessment in Full Rate Production or Full Deployment. See Figure
XII for Cybersecurity Testing by phase requirements. Such activities include, for example, identifying mission-
impacting deficiencies and resolutions, completing residual DT&E actions, performing operational test readiness,
remediation of all missions impacting deficiencies and verification, validation of accreditation for all ranges,
simulations to be involved in the event. The following DoD Guidebooks and Instruction (latest versions) should be
followed for Cybersecurity test and evaluation planning; in addition to specific Service-related guidebooks or
instructions, as appropriate.

         a. DoD Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation Guidebook
         b. Army Regulation 73-1 Test and Evaluation Policy
         c. Director, Operational Test and Evaluation (DOT&E), Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP)
            Guidebook,
         d. DoD Instruction, No. 8510.01; “Risk Management Framework (RMF) for DoD Information
            Technology
         e. DoD Instruction 5000.83 “Technology and Program Protection to Maintain Technological Advantage”
         f. DoD Instruction 5000.90 “Cybersecurity and Acquisition Decision Authorities and Program Managers”

         M.4.1.2.6 Other OT&E Tests 1…n (Specify). Other OT&E tests not captured elsewhere in this
commodity. The following are examples of OT&E tests that may be applicable to this commodity but were
not identified. If pertinent, include those that may be appropriate, by specifically listing them starting with this
WBS element (M.4.2.6) NOTE: Each test should be identified by the next consecutive WBS number (e.g.,
M.4.2.6.1, M.4.2.6.2, etc.)
         a.   Production Verification Test (PVT) and Evaluation
         b.   First Article Test (FAT) and Evaluation
         c.   Comparison test
         d.   Quality Conformance Inspection
         e.   Lot acceptance test
         f.   Post-Deployment Software Support (PDSS) Test
         g.   Surveillance Test
         h.   Reconditioning Test (Pilot or Initial)
         i.   Control Test
         j.   Acceptance Test
         k.   Baseline Evaluation Test
          M.4.1.3 Live Fire Test and Evaluation. The test and evaluation managed by the Program Office and
conducted by dedicated Service laboratories and commands to assess the system’s survivability or lethality
against all operationally representative and relevant threats or targets. It includes test and evaluation conducted
early to identify and mitigate system design vulnerabilities that could affect the mission or force protection
capabilities. It also progresses through OT&E to verify any design changes and to assess the survivability of a
production representative system prior to fielding or full-rate production (FRP). LFT&E includes component,
sub-system, and system-level testing as well as M&S, and evaluation of combat data, safety data, mishap data,
and system designs to:
         a.   Characterize and improve the ability of the system to avoid being hit during its mission. Examples
              include assessment of system signatures, active protection systems or self-defense systems intended
              to mitigate hit probabilities or manipulate hit distributions,
         b.   Identify and mitigate threat induced system design vulnerabilities to improve residual mission
              capability post attack,
         c.   Characterize threat induced crew casualties to identify and implement system design changes that
              could improve force protection in combat,
         d.   Identify and improve system design changes and procedures that could advance system recoverability
              post-combat engagement.

         M.4.1.4 Mock-ups/System Integration Labs (SILs). The design engineering and production of system

                                                         246

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                 MIL-STD-881F
                                                 APPENDIX M

or subsystem mock-ups that have special contractual or engineering significance or that are not required solely
for the conduct of one of the above elements of testing. SILs are often used in lieu of (or in addition to) mock-
ups. SILs are risk reduction facilities where software and hardware can be developed, integrated, tested, and
evaluated for both standalone functionality and/or interoperability prior to being fielded.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Hardware/lab equipment
         b. SIL Software (written to simulate the operating environment or written to operate the SIL)

         M.4.1.5 Test and Evaluation Support. The support elements necessary to operate and maintain, during
test and evaluation, systems and subsystems, which are not consumed during the testing phase and are not
allocated to a specific phase of testing.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Repairable spares, repair of reparables, repair parts, consumables, warehousing and distribution of
             spares and repair parts, test and support equipment, test bed vehicles, drones, surveillance
             aircraft/chase aircraft, tracking vessels, contractor technical support
         Excludes, for example:
         a. Operational and maintenance personnel, special fixtures, special instrumentation, etc., that are
             utilized and/or consumed in a single element of testing and that should be included under that element
             of testing

         M.4.1.6 Test Facilities. The special test facilities required for performance of the various developmental
tests necessary to prove the design and reliability of the system or subsystem.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Test tank test fixtures, propulsion test fixtures, white rooms, test chambers, range/targeting facilities
         b. Cost to the program of developing and/or providing the test capability.

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Brick and mortar-type facilities identified as industrial facilities
         b. Cost to the program for using the capability is not included in this WBS. The cost of providing this
             effort is associated with the WBS it supports (i.e., the actual testing effort).
         M.4.1.6.1 Commercial. Non-Government owned or operated test facilities. Note: Program offices
do need to pay for equipment and usage.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Non-Government ranges/facilities used to test missile weapon System or missile subsystem.
         b. Non-Government laboratories, part of a building, or other place equipped to conduct scientific
             experiments or tests.
         c. Other Test Facilities. Non-Government owned or operated test facilities not listed.
        M.4.1.6.2 Government (Pay-as-You-Go). Government owned or operated test facilities. Note:
Program offices do not pay for the equipment but do need to pay for usage.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Ranges - Government facilities used to test missile weapon System or missile subsystem.
         b. Laboratories - A Government building, part of a building, or other place equipped to conduct
             scientific experiments or tests.
         c. Ranges/Facilities - use of the facility, including equipment brought or provided, “man-hours”, and
             “special test equipment.”
         d. Other Test Facilities - Government owned or operated test facilities not listed.

          M.5.1 System Test and Evaluation. The use of pilot, prototype, production, or specifically fabricated
hardware/software to obtain or validate engineering data on the performance of the system during the
developmental phase of the program. It also includes all effort associated with the development of any specialized
tools or data in support of the system level test program.
          Includes, for example:
          a. Detailed planning, conduct, support, data reduction and reports (excluding the contract data
              requirements list data) from such testing, and test articles that are functionally configured to
              represent and test subsystems/components of the defense materiel end item being developed
              or produced
          b. Design and production of models, specimens, fixtures and instrumentation
          c. System level labs that integrate both hardware and software
          d. Development and/or validation of modeling and simulation resources required for Test and
              Evaluation of a specific system under test.
          e. Training for Government personnel to provide knowledge and capability to deliver test and
              evaluation support actions as required.

NOTE: Test articles that are functionally configured to represent and test the complete defense
materiel end item being developed or produced, are excluded from this WBS element.


         Excludes, for example:
         a. All formal and informal testing up through the subsystem level, which can be associated with the
             hardware/software element acceptance testing

NOTE: These excluded efforts are to be included with the appropriate hardware or software elements.


          M.5.1.1 Developmental Test and Evaluation Developmental Test & Evaluation (DT&E) is
conducted throughout the acquisition process to assist in engineering design and development and to verify
that technical performance specifications have been met. DT&E is planned and monitored by the
developing agency and is normally conducted by the contractor. It includes the T&E of components,
subsystems, Preplanned Product Improvement (P3I) changes, hardware/software integration, and
production qualification testing. It encompasses the use of models, simulations, test beds, and prototypes
or full-scale engineering development models of the system.

         Includes test and evaluation conducted to:
         a. Demonstrate that the engineering design and development process is complete
         b. Demonstrate that the design risks have been minimized
         c. Demonstrate that the system will meet specifications
         d. Assess the system’s military utility when introduced
         e. Determine whether the engineering design is supportable (practical, maintainable, safe, etc.) for
              operational use
         f. Provide test data with which to examine and evaluate trade-offs against specification requirements,
              life cycle cost, and schedule
         g. Perform the logistics testing efforts to evaluate the achievement of supportability goals, the
              adequacy of the support package for the system, (e.g., deliverable maintenance tools, test
              equipment, technical publications, maintenance instructions, and personnel skills and training
              requirements, etc.)
         M.5.1.1.1 Engineering Development Test. Developmental testing on an engineering unit, early model, or
release of a product built to test a concept or process or to act as an item to be replicated or learned from.

          M.5.1.1.2 System Qualification Test. System Qualification Test to ensure that the integrated system is
tested to provide evidence for compliance with the system requirements and that the system is ready for delivery.

          M 5.1.1.3 Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation. The developmental test and evaluation (DT&E) conducted
and monitored by the developing agency of the DoD. This will determine the DT&E results for Low Rate Initial
Production and the Limited Deployment Phase associated with Cooperative Vulnerability Identification and
Adversarial Cybersecurity. See Figure XII for Cybersecurity Testing by phase requirements. Such activities include,
for example, developing cybersecurity test objectives, testing security standards, operational resilience, and system
cyber survivability. Also includes planning cybersecurity test infrastructure, adversarial DT&E, and test operational
resistance. The following DoD Guidebooks and Instruction (latest versions) should be followed for Cybersecurity
test and evaluation planning; in addition to specific Service-related guidebooks or instructions, as appropriate.
         a.   DoD Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation Guidebook
         b.   Army Regulation 73-1 Test and Evaluation Policy
         c.   Director, Operational Test and Evaluation (DOT&E), Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP)
              Guidebook,
         d.   DoD Instruction, No. 8510.01; “Risk Management Framework (RMF) for DoD Information
              Technology”
         e.   DoD Instruction 5000.83 “Technology and Program Protection to Maintain Technological
              Advantage”
         f.   DoD Instruction 5000.90 “Cybersecurity and Acquisition Decision Authorities and Program

           M.5.1.2.1 Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation. The operational test and evaluation (OT&E) conducted and
monitored by DoD agencies. The technical efforts of ensuring the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of
information and technology by monitoring and defending against cyber (network) attacks. Early operational testing
(that is, EUT and LUT), as well as Initial Operation and Test (IOT) will determine the OT&E results for Vulnerability
and Penetration Assessment and Adversarial Assessment in Full Rate Production or Full Deployment. See Figure
XII for Cybersecurity Testing by phase requirements. Such activities include, for example, identifying mission-
impacting deficiencies and resolutions, completing residual DT&E actions, performing operational test readiness,
remediation of all missions impacting deficiencies and verification, validation of accreditation for all ranges,
simulations to be involved in the event. The following DoD Guidebooks and Instruction (latest versions) should be

              Technology”

              Advantage”
         f.   DoD Instruction 5000.90 “Cybersecurity and Acquisition Decision Authorities and Program
              Managers”

WBS element (M.5.2.2) NOTE: Each test should be identified by the next consecutive WBS number (e.g.,

         a.   Production Verification Test (PVT) and Evaluation
         b.   First Article Test (FAT) and Evaluation

         k.   Baseline Evaluation Test
         l.   Regression Test (unique to software-intensive systems.)

          M.5.1.3 Live Fire Test and Evaluation. The test and evaluation managed by the Program Office and
conducted by dedicated Service laboratories and commands to assess the system’s survivability or lethality
against all operationally representative and relevant threats or targets. It includes test and evaluation conducted
early to identify and mitigate system design vulnerabilities that could affect the mission or force protection
capabilities. It also progresses through OT&E to verify any design changes and to assess the survivability of a
production representative system prior to fielding or full-rate production (FRP). LFT&E includes component,
sub-system, and system-level testing as well as M&S, and evaluation of combat data, safety data, mishap data,
and system designs to:

         a.   Characterize and improve the ability of the system to avoid being hit during its mission. Examples
              include assessment of system signatures, active protection systems or self-defense systems intended
              to mitigate hit probabilities or manipulate hit distributions,
         b.   Identify and mitigate threat induced system design vulnerabilities to improve residual mission
              capability post attack,
         c.   Characterize threat induced crew casualties to identify and implement system design changes that
              could improve force protection in combat,
         d.   Identify and improve system design changes and procedures that could advance system
              recoverability post-combat engagement.

         M.5.1.4 Mock-ups/System Integration Labs (SILs). The design engineering and production of system
or subsystem mock-ups that have special contractual or engineering significance or that are not required solely
for the conduct of one of the above elements of testing. SILs are often used in lieu of (or in addition to) mock-
ups. SILs are risk reduction facilities where software and hardware can be developed, integrated, tested, and
evaluated for both standalone functionality and/or interoperability prior to being fielded.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Hardware/lab equipment
         b. SIL Software (written to simulate the operating environment or written to operate the SIL)

         M.5.1.5 Test and Evaluation Support. The support elements necessary to operate and maintain, during
test and evaluation, systems and subsystems, which are not consumed during the testing phase and are not
allocated to a specific phase of testing.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Repairable spares, repair of reparables, repair parts, consumables, warehousing and distribution of
              spares and repair parts, test and support equipment, test bed vehicles, drones, surveillance
              aircraft/chase aircraft, tracking vessels, contractor technical support

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Operational and maintenance personnel, special fixtures, special instrumentation, etc., that are
             utilized and/or consumed in a single element of testing and that should be included under that element
             of testing

          M.5.1.6 Test Facilities. The special test facilities required for performance of the various developmental
tests necessary to prove the design and reliability of the system or subsystem.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Test tank test fixtures, propulsion test fixtures, white rooms, test chambers, range/targeting facilities
         b. Cost to the program of developing and/or providing the test capability.

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Brick and mortar-type facilities identified as industrial facilities
         b. Cost to the program for using the capability is not included in this WBS. The cost of providing this
             effort is associated with the WBS it supports (i.e., the actual testing effort).

                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                               MIL-STD-881F
                                               APPENDIX M

         M.5.1.6.1 Commercial. Non-Government owned or operated test facilities. Note: Program offices
do pay for the equipment and for usage.

        Includes, for example:
        a. Non-Government ranges/facilities used to test missile weapon System or missile subsystem.
        b. Non-Government laboratories, part of a building, or other place equipped to conduct scientific
            experiments or tests.
        c. Other Test Facilities. Non-Government owned or operated test facilities not listed.
        M.5.1.6.2 Government (Pay-as-You-Go). Government owned or operated test facilities. Note:
Program offices do not pay for the equipment but do need to pay for usage.
        Includes, for example:
        a. Ranges - Government facilities used to test missile weapon System or missile subsystem.
        b. Laboratories - A Government building, part of a building, or other place equipped to conduct
            scientific experiments or tests.
        c. Ranges/Facilities - use of the facility, including equipment brought or provided, “man-hours”, and
            “special test equipment.”
        d. Other Test Facilities - Government owned or operated test facilities not listed.

                                     MISSILES / ORDNANCE SYSTEMS

M.6 Work Breakdown Structure Levels and Definitions for Government System Test and Evaluation

WORK BREAKDOWN STRUCTURE LEVELS

         M.6.1 System Test and Evaluation. The use of pilot, prototype, production, or specifically
fabricated hardware/software to obtain or validate engineering data on the performance of the system during
the developmental phase of the program. It also includes all effort associated with the development of any
specialized tools or data in support of the system level test program.
          Includes, for example:
          a. Detailed planning, conduct, support, data reduction and reports (excluding the contract data
              requirements list data) from such testing, and test articles that are functionally configured to
              represent and test subsystems/components of the defense materiel end item being developed
              or produced
          b. Design and production of models, specimens, fixtures and instrumentation
          c. System level labs that integrate both hardware and software
          d. Development and/or validation of modeling and simulation resources required for Test and

                                                         254

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                 MIL-STD-881F
                                                 APPENDIX M

              Evaluation of a specific system under test.
         e.   Training for Government personnel to provide knowledge and capability to deliver test and
              evaluation support actions as required.

NOTE: Test articles that are functionally configured to represent and test the complete defense
materiel end item being developed or produced, are excluded from this WBS element.


         Excludes, for example:
         a. All formal and informal testing up through the subsystem level, which can be associated with the
             hardware/software element acceptance testing

NOTE: These excluded efforts are to be included with the appropriate hardware or software elements.


          M.6.1.1 Developmental Test and Evaluation. Developmental Test & Evaluation (DT&E) is
conducted throughout the acquisition process to assist in engineering design and development and to verify
that technical performance specifications have been met. DT&E is planned and monitored by the
developing agency and is normally conducted by the contractor. It includes the T&E of components,
subsystems, Preplanned Product Improvement (P3I) changes, hardware/software integration, and
production qualification testing. It encompasses the use of models, simulations, test beds, and prototypes
or full-scale engineering development models of the system.
         Includes test and evaluation conducted to:
         a. Demonstrate that the engineering design and development process is complete
         b. Demonstrate that the design risks have been minimized
         c. Demonstrate that the system will meet specifications
         d. Assess the system’s military utility when introduced
         e. Determine whether the engineering design is supportable (practical, maintainable, safe, etc.) for
              operational use
         f. Provide test data with which to examine and evaluate trade-offs against specification requirements,
              life cycle cost, and schedule
         g. Perform the logistics testing efforts to evaluate the achievement of supportability goals, the
              adequacy of the support package for the system, (e.g., deliverable maintenance tools, test
              equipment, technical publications, maintenance instructions, and personnel skills and training
              requirements, etc.)

         M.6.1.1.1 Engineering Development Test. Developmental testing on an engineering unit, early model, or
release of a product built to test a concept or process or to act as an item to be replicated or learned from.

          M.6.1.1.2 System Qualification Test. System Qualification Test to ensure that the integrated system is
tested to provide evidence for compliance with the system requirements and that the system is ready for delivery.

         M.6.1.1.3 Logistics Demonstration (LD). LD addresses the achievement of maintainability goals of the
system hardware and the adequacy and sustainability of tools, test equipment, selected test programs sets, built-in
test equipment, and associated support items of equipment, technical publications, and maintenance instructions.
It examines the adequacy of trouble-shooting procedures; personnel skill requirements; selection and allocation of
spare parts, tools, test equipment, and tasks to appropriate maintenance levels; and adequacy of maintenance time
standards. shooting procedures; personnel skill requirements; selection and allocation of spare parts, tools, test
equipment, and tasks to appropriate maintenance levels.

         M.6.1.1.4 Interoperability Certification Test. A test that applies to command, control, communications,
computers, and IT systems that has interfaces or interoperability requirements with other systems. This test may
consist of simple demonstrations with limited connectivity using message analysis or parsing software with limited
interface connectivity or extend to full-scale scenario-driven exercises with all interfaces connected.


                                                        255

                           Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                         Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                  MIL-STD-881F
                                                  APPENDIX M

         M.6.1.1.5 Test Instrumentation Kits (TIK). The Test Instrumentation Kit (TIK) provides telemetry of the
missile in-flight data parameters, provides for destructive flight by Range Safety, and provides a transponder for
test range tracking.

          M.6.1.1.6 Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation. The developmental test and evaluation (DT&E) conducted
and monitored by the developing agency of the DoD. This will determine the DT&E results for Low Rate Initial
Production and the Limited Deployment Phase associated with Cooperative Vulnerability Identification and
Adversarial Cybersecurity. See Figure XII for Cybersecurity Testing by phase requirements. Such activities include,
for example, developing cybersecurity test objectives, testing security standards, operational resilience, and system
cyber survivability. Also includes planning cybersecurity test infrastructure, adversarial DT&E, and test operational
resistance. The following DoD Guidebooks and Instruction (latest versions) should be followed for Cybersecurity
test and evaluation planning; in addition to specific Service-related guidebooks or instructions, as appropriate.

         a.   DoD Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation Guidebook
         b.   Army Regulation 73-1 Test and Evaluation Policy
         c.   Director, Operational Test and Evaluation (DOT&E), Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP)
              Guidebook,
         d.   DoD Instruction, No. 8510.01; “Risk Management Framework (RMF) for DoD Information
              Technology”
         e.   DoD Instruction 5000.83 “Technology and Program Protection to Maintain Technological
              Advantage”
         f.   DoD Instruction 5000.90 “Cybersecurity and Acquisition Decision Authorities and Program
              Managers”

          M.6.1.1.7 Service/Agency Other DT&E Tests 1...n (Specify). The costs to rent, lease, procure, or utilize
a military weapon system in order to support the testing of a different, primary weapon system. These costs are
normally paid to by the primary weapon system program being tested to another program office/program in order
to utilize equipment or systems needed to test or characterize the primary weapon system.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Costs to utilize a ship, aircraft, ground vehicle used to act as an opposing force (e.g., “red team”).
         b. Costs to repair or demilitarize a non-primary military system damaged during the testing of the
             primary weapon system.

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Costs to procure, repair, or demilitarize the primary weapon system being tested.

         M.6.1.1.8 Other DT&E Tests 1…n (Specify). Other DT&E tests not captured elsewhere in this
commodity. The following are examples of DT&E tests that may be applicable to this commodity but were
not identified. If pertinent, include those that may be appropriate, by specifically listing them by starting with
this WBS element (M.6.1.8) NOTE: Each test should be identified by the next consecutive WBS number (e.g.,
M.6.1.8.1, M.6.1.8.2, etc.).

         Examples of Developmental Tests
         a. Market Research effort (or proof of concept test) and Evaluation
         b. Technical Feasibility Test (TFT) and Evaluation
         c. Engineering development test (EDT) and Evaluation
         d. Production prove-out test (PPT) and Evaluation
         e. Production qualification Test (PQT) and Evaluation
         f. Software development test (SDT) and Evaluation
         g. Software qualification test (SQT) and Evaluation
         h. Interoperability certification test and Evaluation
         i. Soldier protection testing and Evaluation
         j. Technical Manual (TM) Verification
         k. Physical configuration audit (PCA)/Functional configuration audit (FCA)

                                                    MIL-STD-881F
                                                    APPENDIX M

         Examples of Pre-Operational Tests
         a. Production Verification Test (PVT) and Evaluation
         b. First Article Test (FAT) and Evaluation
         c. Comparison test
         d. Quality Conformance Inspection
         e. Lot acceptance test
         f. Post-Deployment Software Support (PDSS) Test
         g. Surveillance Test
         h. Reconditioning Test (Pilot or Initial)
         i. Control Test
         j. Acceptance Test
         k. Baseline Evaluation Test
         l. Regression Test (unique to software-intensive systems)

          M.6.1.2 Operational Test and Evaluation. The test and evaluation conducted by agencies other than the
developing command to assess the prospective system’s military utility, operational effectiveness, operational
suitability, logistics supportability (including compatibility, inter-operability, reliability, maintainability, logistic
requirements, etc.) The system is also being tested against the operational doctrine suitable for the system against
the threats expected to be encountered by the system, cost of ownership, and need for any modifications.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Initial operational test and evaluation conducted during the development of a system
         b. Such tests as system demonstration, flight tests, sea trials, mobility demonstrations, on-orbit tests,
             spin demonstration, stability tests, qualification operational test and evaluation, etc., and support
             thereto, required to prove the operational capability of the deliverable system.
         c. Contractor support (e.g., technical assistance, maintenance, labor, material, etc.) consumed during
             this phase of testing
         d. Logistics testing efforts to evaluate the achievement of supportability goals and the adequacy of
             the support for the system (e.g., deliverable maintenance tools, test equipment, technical
             publications, maintenance instructions, personnel skills and training requirements, and software
             support facility/environment elements).
         e. Pre-full-rate production/full deployment tests - Common to all systems, including (1) Early User Test
             (EUT). (2) Limited User Test (LUT). (3) Initial Operating Test (IOT).
         f. Post-full-rate production/full deployment tests, including: (1) Full Operational Test (FOT). (2) User
             acceptance test (UAT) and unique to IT systems test.
         g. As-required tests, including (1) Customer test (CT) (common to all systems). (2) Supplemental site
             test (SST) (unique to IT systems). (3) Regression test (unique to software-intensive systems).

           M.6.1.2.1 Cybersecurity System and Evaluation. The operational test and evaluation (OT&E) conducted
and monitored by DoD agencies. The technical efforts of ensuring the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of
information and technology by monitoring and defending against cyber (network) attacks. Early operational testing
(that is, EUT and LUT), as well as Initial Operation and Test (IOT) will determine the OT&E results for Vulnerability
and Penetration Assessment and Adversarial Assessment in Full Rate Production or Full Deployment. See Figure
XII for Cybersecurity Testing by phase requirements. Such activities include, for example, identifying mission-
impacting deficiencies and resolutions, completing residual DT&E actions, performing operational test readiness,
remediation of all missions impacting deficiencies and verification, validation of accreditation for all ranges,
simulations to be involved in the event. The following DoD Guidebooks and Instruction (latest versions) should be
followed for Cybersecurity test and evaluation planning; in addition to specific Service-related guidebooks or
instructions, as appropriate.

         a.   DoD Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation Guidebook
         b.   Army Regulation 73-1 Test and Evaluation Policy
         c.   Director, Operational Test and Evaluation (DOT&E), Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP)
              Guidebook,
         d.   DoD Instruction, No. 8510.01; “Risk Management Framework (RMF) for DoD Information
              Technology”

         e.   DoD Instruction 5000.83 “Technology and Program Protection to Maintain Technological
              Advantage”
         f.   DoD Instruction 5000.90 “Cybersecurity and Acquisition Decision Authorities and Program
              Managers”

         M.6.1.2.2 Other OT&E Tests 1…n (Specify). Other OT&E tests not captured elsewhere in this
commodity. The following are examples of OT&E tests that may be applicable to this commodity but were
not identified. If pertinent, include those that may be appropriate, by specifically listing them under this WBS
element (M.6.2.2) NOTE: Each test should be identified by the next consecutive WBS number (e.g., M.6.2.2.1,
M.6.2.2.2, etc.)

         a.   Production Verification Test (PVT) and Evaluation
         b.   First Article Test (FAT) and Evaluation
         c.   Comparison test
         d.   Quality Conformance Inspection
         e.   Lot acceptance test

         g.   Surveillance Test
         h.   Reconditioning Test (Pilot or Initial)
         i.   Control Test
         j.   Acceptance Test
         k.   Baseline Evaluation Test

          M.6.1.3 Live Fire Test and Evaluation. The test and evaluation managed by the Program Office and
conducted by dedicated Service laboratories and commands to assess the system’s survivability or lethality
against all operationally representative and relevant threats or targets. It includes test and evaluation conducted
early to identify and mitigate system design vulnerabilities that could affect the mission or force protection
capabilities. It also progresses through OT&E to verify any design changes and to assess the survivability of a
production representative system prior to fielding or full-rate production (FRP). LFT&E includes component,
sub-system, and system-level testing as well as M&S, and evaluation of combat data, safety data, mishap data,
and system designs to:
         a.   Characterize and improve the ability of the system to avoid being hit during its mission. Examples
              include assessment of system signatures, active protection systems or self-defense systems intended
              to mitigate hit probabilities or manipulate hit distributions,
         b.   Identify and mitigate threat induced system design vulnerabilities to improve residual mission
              capability post attack,
         c.   Characterize threat induced crew casualties to identify and implement system design changes that
              could improve force protection in combat,
         d.   Identify and improve system design changes and procedures that could advance system recoverability
              post-combat engagement.

         M.6.1.4 Mock-ups/System Integration Labs (SILs). The design engineering and production of system or
subsystem mock-ups that have special contractual or engineering significance or that are not required solely for
the conduct of one of the above elements of testing. SILs are often used in lieu of (or in addition to) mock-ups.
SILs are risk reduction facilities where software and hardware can be developed, integrated, tested, and evaluated
for both standalone functionality and/or interoperability prior to being fielded.

          M.6.1.5 Test and Evaluation Support. The support elements necessary to operate and maintain, during
test and evaluation, systems and subsystems, which are not consumed during the testing phase and are not allocated
to a specific phase of testing.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Repairable spares, repair of reparables, repair parts, consumables, warehousing and distribution of
             spares and repair parts, test and support equipment, test bed vehicles, drones, surveillance
             aircraft/chase aircraft, tracking vessels, contractor technical support

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Operational and maintenance personnel, special fixtures, special instrumentation, etc., that are utilized
             and/or consumed in a single element of testing and that should be included under that element of testing

         M.6.1.6 Test Facilities. The special test facilities required for performance of the various developmental
tests necessary to prove the design and reliability of the system or subsystem.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Test tank test fixtures, propulsion test fixtures, white rooms, test chambers, range/targeting facilities

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Brick and mortar-type facilities identified as industrial facilities
         b. Cost to the program for using the capability is not included in this WBS. The cost of providing this
             effort is associated with the WBS it supports (i.e., the actual testing effort).

         M.6.1.6.1 Commercial. Non-Government owned or operated test facilities. Note: Program offices

             scientific experiments or tests.
         c. Ranges/Facilities - use of the facility, including equipment brought or provided, “man-hours”, and
             “special test equipment.”
         d. Other Test Facilities - Government owned or operated test facilities not listed.

fabricated hardware/software to obtain or validate engineering data on the performance of the system during
the developmental phase of the program. It also includes all effort associated with the development of any
specialized tools or data in support of the system level test program.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Detailed planning, conduct, support, data reduction and reports (excluding the contract data
             requirements list data) from such testing, and test articles that are functionally configured to
             represent and test subsystems/components of the defense materiel end item being developed
             or produced
         b. Design and production of models, specimens, fixtures and instrumentation

         a. All formal and informal testing up through the subsystem level, which can be associated with the
             hardware/software element acceptance testing

          M.7.1.1 Developmental Test and Evaluation. Developmental Test & Evaluation (DT&E) is
conducted throughout the acquisition process to assist in engineering design and development and to verify
that technical performance specifications have been met. DT&E is planned and monitored by the
developing agency and is normally conducted by the contractor. It includes the T&E of components,
subsystems, Preplanned Product Improvement (P3I) changes, hardware/software integration, and
production qualification testing. It encompasses the use of models, simulations, test beds, and prototypes
or full-scale engineering development models of the system.

         Includes test and evaluation conducted to:
         a. Demonstrate that the engineering design and development process is complete
         b. Demonstrate that the design risks have been minimized
         c. Demonstrate that the system will meet specifications
         d. Assess the system’s military utility when introduced
         e. Determine whether the engineering design is supportable (practical, maintainable, safe, etc.) for
              operational use
         f. Provide test data with which to examine and evaluate trade-offs against specification requirements,
              life cycle cost, and schedule
         g. Perform the logistics testing efforts to evaluate the achievement of supportability goals, the
              adequacy of the support package for the system, (e.g., deliverable maintenance tools, test
              equipment, technical publications, maintenance instructions, and personnel skills and training
              requirements, etc.)

         Includes, for example:
         a. All contractor/system developer in-house effort
         b. Where applicable, models, tests and associated simulations (e.g., such as wind tunnel, static, drop,
             and fatigue); integration ground tests; test bed aircraft and associated support; qualification test and
             evaluation, development flight test, test instrumentation, environmental tests, ballistics, radiological,

              range and accuracy demonstrations, test facility operations, test equipment (including its support
              equipment), chase and calibrated pacer aircraft and support thereto, and logistics testing
         c.   Test articles such as inert measurement vehicles, launch separation vehicles, separation and control
              test vehicles, boost test vehicles
         d.   Target/Threat Simulator testing to evaluate target/threat systems, tactics, and overall capabilities to
              yield credible, valid results of a system’s performance in a realistic operational environment.

         M.7.1.1.1 Prototype (Pathfinder) Testing. Developmental testing on an early model, or release of a
product built to test a concept or process or to act as an item to be replicated or learned from.

          M.7.1.1.2 Qualification Test (X & PEM). Systematic examination performed to ascertain if a system or
item is capable of meeting necessary requirements. (PEM is term for Performance/Production Evaluation Missiles.)
Includes all efforts of technical engineering activities associated with a robust testing program to demonstrate that
the design, manufacturing process, and acceptance program produce hardware meeting specification requirements.
Ensures all related Systems interact seamlessly, verifying a system’s ability to operate as expected with other
Systems within the same environment.

         M.7.1.1.3 Static Testing. Testing the missile propulsion system in a fixed test stand on the ground. The
effort includes all activities required to evaluate the performance of the booster static fire testing during the
development phase.

         M.7.1.1.3.1 AUR Mechanical/Load Testing. This effort includes all activities to support mechanical/loads
testing during the development phase.

         M.7.1.1.3.2 AUR Environmental Testing. This effort includes all activities to support environmental
testing during the development phase.

         M.7.1.1.4 System Integration Testing. System Integration Testing requires close coordination across all
streams/applications and makes sure that all Systems have been integrated successfully by verifying the interfaces
between the System, ensuring the system that might consist of multiple sub-System to produce the expected result.

         M.7.1.1.5 System Acceptance Testing. Testing conducted to determine if the requirements of a
specification or contract are met. It may involve chemical tests, physical tests, or performance tests. This WBS
element provides the resources for the planning, installation, and check-out of missile upgrades to the test, training,
and operational environments.

          M.7.1.1.6 Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation The developmental test and evaluation (DT&E) conducted
and monitored by the developing agency of the DoD. This will determine the DT&E results for Low Rate Initial
Production and the Limited Deployment Phase associated with Cooperative Vulnerability Identification and
Adversarial Cybersecurity. See Figure XII for Cybersecurity Testing by phase requirements. Such activities include,
for example, developing cybersecurity test objectives, testing security standards, operational resilience, and system
cyber survivability. Also includes planning cybersecurity test infrastructure, adversarial DT&E, and test operational
resistance. The following DoD Guidebooks and Instruction (latest versions) should be followed for Cybersecurity
test and evaluation planning; in addition to specific Service-related guidebooks or instructions, as appropriate.

         a.   DoD Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation Guidebook
         b.   Army Regulation 73-1 Test and Evaluation Policy
         c.   Director, Operational Test and Evaluation (DOT&E), Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP)
              Guidebook,
         d.   DoD Instruction, No. 8510.01; “Risk Management Framework (RMF) for DoD Information
              Technology”
         e.   DoD Instruction 5000.83 “Technology and Program Protection to Maintain Technological
              Advantage”
         f.   DoD Instruction 5000.90 “Cybersecurity and Acquisition Decision Authorities and Program
              Managers”


                                                         262

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                    MIL-STD-881F
                                                    APPENDIX M

         M.7.1.1.7 Service/Agency DT&E 1...n (Specify). The costs to rent, lease, procure, or utilize a military
weapon system in order to support the testing of a different, primary weapon system. These costs are normally
paid for by the primary weapon system program being tested to another program office/program in order to utilize
equipment or systems needed to test or characterize the primary weapon system.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Costs to utilize a ship, aircraft, ground vehicle used to act as an opposing force (e.g., “red team”).
         b. Costs to repair or demilitarize a non-primary military system damaged during the testing of the
             primary weapon system.

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Costs to procure, repair, or demilitarize the primary weapon system being tested

         M.7.1.1.8 Other DT&E Tests 1…n (Specify). Other DT&E tests not captured elsewhere in this
commodity. The following are examples of DT&E tests that may be applicable to this commodity but were
not identified. If pertinent, include those that may be appropriate, by specifically listing them by starting with
this WBS element (M.7.1.8) NOTE: Each test should be identified by the next consecutive WBS number (e.g.,
M.7.1.8.1, M.7.1.8.2, etc.).

         Examples of Developmental Tests
         a. Market Research effort (or proof of concept test) and Evaluation
         b. Technical Feasibility Test (TFT) and Evaluation
         c. Engineering development test (EDT) and Evaluation
         d. Production prove-out test (PPT) and Evaluation
         e. Production qualification Test (PQT) and Evaluation
         f. Software development test (SDT) and Evaluation
         g. Software qualification test (SQT) and Evaluation
         h. Interoperability certification test and Evaluation
         i. Soldier protection testing and Evaluation
         j. Technical Manual (TM) Verification
         k. Physical configuration audit (PCA)/Functional configuration audit (FCA)

         Examples of Pre-Operational Tests
         a. Production Verification Test (PVT) and Evaluation
         b. First Article Test (FAT) and Evaluation
         c. Comparison test
         d. Quality Conformance Inspection
         e. Lot acceptance test
         f. Post-Deployment Software Support (PDSS) Test
         g. Surveillance Test
         h. Reconditioning Test (Pilot or Initial)
         i. Control Test
         j. Acceptance Test
         k. Baseline Evaluation Test

          M.7.1.2 Operational Test and Evaluation. The test and evaluation conducted by agencies other than the
developing command to assess the prospective system’s military utility, operational effectiveness, operational
suitability, logistics supportability (including compatibility, inter-operability, reliability, maintainability, logistic
requirements, etc.) The system is also being tested against the operational doctrine suitable for the system against
the threats expected to be encountered by the system, cost of ownership, and need for any modifications.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Initial operational test and evaluation conducted during the development of a system
         b. Such tests as system demonstration, flight tests, sea trials, mobility demonstrations, on-orbit tests,
             spin demonstration, stability tests, qualification operational test and evaluation, etc., and support


                                                           263

                             Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                           Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                  MIL-STD-881F
                                                  APPENDIX M

              thereto, required to prove the operational capability of the deliverable system.
         c.   Contractor support (e.g., technical assistance, maintenance, labor, material, etc.) consumed during
              this phase of testing
         d.   Logistics testing efforts to evaluate the achievement of supportability goals and the adequacy of
              the support for the system (e.g., deliverable maintenance tools, test equipment, technical
              publications, maintenance instructions, personnel skills and training requirements, and software
              support facility/environment elements).
         e.   Pre-full-rate production/full deployment tests - Common to all systems, including (1) Early User Test
              (EUT). (2) Limited User Test (LUT). (3) Initial Operating Test (IOT).
         f.   Post-full-rate production/full deployment tests, including: (1) Full Operational Test (FOT). (2) User
              acceptance test (UAT) and unique to IT systems test.
         g.   As-required tests, including (1) Customer test (CT) (common to all systems). (2) Supplemental site
              test (SST) (unique to IT systems). (3) Regression test (unique to software-intensive systems).

          M.7.1.2.1 Initial Commander Evaluation Test. An initial determination of reliability and accuracy. The
requirements dictate the confidence with which reliability and accuracy must be determined, which when coupled
with the estimated or measured reliability and accuracy, dictate the number of flight tests required to achieve these
confidence limits. The initial determination confidence limit is very stringent, requiring a larger number of initial
flight tests to accomplish the requirements.

         M.7.1.2.2 Ground/Simulated Electronic Launch. Purpose is to thoroughly test the weapon system
ensuring a reliable, safe, secure and effective weapon system without actual launches. Done with launch sites in
simulation mode. The technical efforts include Computer in the Loop (CIL) and Hardware in the Loop (HIL) test
resources, test planning, test integration, test execution, test analysis and reporting in order to efficiently complete
validation/verification activities. Helps ensure that the launch facilities and missiles, as well as launch control
centers are operationally effective.

          M.7.1.2.2.1 GTI Integration Event 1…n (Specify). This element provides for the planning, analysis and
reporting for the execution and support of simulation testing for each event. This includes conducting the ground
test integration, including allocating resources. The plan contains a detailed understanding of the test flow.

        M.7.1.2.2.2 GTD Demonstration Event 1…n (Specify). This element provides for the planning, analysis
and reporting for the execution and support of simulation testing for each event. The technical efforts required for
conducting the ground test demonstration, including allocating resources. The plan contains a detailed
understanding of the test flow.

          M.7.1.2.3 Flight Testing 1…n (Specify). Extensive and continuous throughout the deployed
lifetime of the weapon system, as is required for all nuclear capable weapon systems.

        M.7.1.2.3.1 Build Verification Testing, Controls Validation Test, or Flight Test Ground-Based
1…n (Specify). This element provides for the planning, execution, analysis and reporting for the execution
and support of flight testing for each event.

         M.7.1.2.3.1.1 Mission Planning and Integration. The technical efforts required to plan for
conducting the verification event, including allocating resources. The plan contains a detailed understanding
of the specific test flow, flight testing, and test objectives. Also includes the technical efforts required to
conduct Test Integration tasks including, but not limited to providing pre-test coordination including
supporting, planning, meetings/reviews, document interim and final test plan including test requirements,
schedule, and test configuration.

          M.7.1.2.3.1.2 Launch Operations. Includes all tasks related to preparation for launch at the launch
facility. Activities include launch preparation and management, preparing for and performing launch
operations, and activities performed at the launch site after the launch is complete.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Launch Operations Planning,

                                                         264

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                 MIL-STD-881F
                                                 APPENDIX M

         b. Launch Operations Campaign
         c. Launch Site Retrograde
         d. Launch Site and Equipment Refurbishment
         e. Transportation

           M..7.1.2.4 Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation. The operational test and evaluation (OT&E) conducted and
monitored by DoD agencies. The technical efforts of ensuring the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of
information and technology by monitoring and defending against cyber (network) attacks. Early operational testing
(that is, EUT and LUT), as well as Initial Operation and Test (IOT) will determine the OT&E results for Vulnerability
and Penetration Assessment and Adversarial Assessment in Full Rate Production or Full Deployment. See Figure
XII for Cybersecurity Testing by phase requirements. Such activities include, for example, identifying mission-
impacting deficiencies and resolutions, completing residual DT&E actions, performing operational test readiness,
remediation of all missions impacting deficiencies and verification, validation of accreditation for all ranges,
simulations to be involved in the event. The following DoD Guidebooks and Instruction (latest versions) should be
followed for Cybersecurity test and evaluation planning; in addition to specific Service-related guidebooks or
instructions, as appropriate.

         a.   DoD Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation Guidebook
         b.   Army Regulation 73-1 Test and Evaluation Policy
         c.   Director, Operational Test and Evaluation (DOT&E), Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP)
              Guidebook,
         d.   DoD Instruction, No. 8510.01; “Risk Management Framework (RMF) for DoD Information
              Technology”
         e.   DoD Instruction 5000.83 “Technology and Program Protection to Maintain Technological
              Advantage”
         f.   DoD Instruction 5000.90 “Cybersecurity and Acquisition Decision Authorities and Program
              Managers”

        M.7.1.2.5 Contractor Support 1…n (Specify). Contractor efforts to help plan, coordinate, conduct,
and document OT&E test results.

         M.7.1.2.6 OT&E Software Release 1…n (Specify). The resources associated with PMP software that
are not associated with a specific PMP subsystem (i.e., distributed software environment) for release (1...n).

         Includes, for example:
         a. Computer Software Configuration Item (CSCI) 1...n (Specify). OT&E Computer Software
             Configuration Items (CSCIs) are aggregations of software or any of its discrete portions that
             satisfies an end use function and has been designated by the Government or Contractor, if the
             Government did not specify, for configuration management. CSCIs are the major software
             products of a system acquisition, which are developed in accordance with standard DoD or
             commercial practices.
         b. Software Integration, Assembly, Test, and Checkout

         M.7.1.2.7 Other OT&E Tests 1…n (Specify). Other OT&E tests not captured elsewhere in this
commodity. The following are examples of OT&E tests that may be applicable to this commodity but were
not identified. If pertinent, include those that may be appropriate, by specifically listing them under this WBS
element (M.7.2.7) NOTE: Each test should be identified by the next consecutive WBS number (e.g., M.7.2.7.1,
M.7.2.7.2, etc.)

         a.   Production Verification Test (PVT) and Evaluation
         b.   First Article Test (FAT) and Evaluation
         c.   Comparison test
         d.   Quality Conformance Inspection
         e.   Lot acceptance test
         f.   Post-Deployment Software Support (PDSS) Test
         g.   Surveillance Test

         h.   Reconditioning Test (Pilot or Initial)
         i.   Control Test
         j.   Acceptance Test
         k.   Baseline Evaluation Test

         M.7.1.3 Mock-ups/System Integration Labs (SILs). The design engineering and production of system
or subsystem mock-ups that have special contractual or engineering significance or that are not required solely
for the conduct of one of the above elements of testing. SILs are often used in lieu of (or in addition to) mock-
ups. SILs are risk reduction facilities where software and hardware can be developed, integrated, tested, and
evaluated for both standalone functionality and/or interoperability prior to being fielded.

          M.7.1.4 Test and Evaluation Support. The support elements necessary to operate and maintain, during
test and evaluation, systems and subsystems, which are not consumed during the testing phase and are not allocated
to a specific phase of testing.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Repairable spares, repair of reparables, repair parts, consumables, warehousing and distribution of
             spares and repair parts, test and support equipment, test bed vehicles, drones, surveillance
             aircraft/chase aircraft, tracking vessels, contractor technical support.
         Excludes, for example:
         a. Operational and maintenance personnel, special fixtures, special instrumentation, etc., that are
             utilized and/or consumed in a single element of testing and that should be included under that element
             of testing.
        M.7.1.4.1 Test Planning. Efforts associated with documenting the strategy that will be used to verify
and ensure that a product or system meets its design specifications and other requirements.

         M.7.1.4.2 Pretest Activities. Test related efforts undertaken prior to the start of the testing.

         M.7.1.4.3 Post-Test Analysis. Analyzing real-time test results from test events and experiments.

         M.7.1.5 Test Facilities. The special test facilities required for performance of the various developmental
tests necessary to prove the design and reliability of the system or subsystem.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Test tank test fixtures, propulsion test fixtures, white rooms, test chambers, range/targeting facilities
         b. Cost to the program of developing and/or providing the test capability.

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Brick and mortar-type facilities identified as industrial facilities
         b. Cost to the program for using the capability is not included in this WBS. The cost of providing this
             effort is associated with the WBS it supports (i.e., the actual testing effort).

         M.7.1.5.1 Test Equipment. Range equipment required to perform a test.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Test Support Equipment - Deliverable items and associated software required to support and
             maintain the system or portions of the system while the system is in test.
         b. Unique Test Equipment - Single or multipurpose integrated test units engineered, designed,
             fabricated or modified to accomplish special purpose testing in performing a contract.

        M.7.1.5.2 Instrumentation Testing. Monitoring and recording equipment used to monitor various
parameters.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Development Test

        b.   Flight Test Instrumentation
        c.   Post Test
        d.   System Level Test
        e.   Instrumentation Testing Software Release 1…n (Specify)
             i. Instrumentation testing computer software Configuration Items (CSCIs) are aggregations of
                software of any of its discrete portions that satisfies an end use function and has been designated by
                the Government or Contractor, if the Government did not specify, for configuration management.
                CSCIs are the major software products of a system acquisition, which are developed in accordance
                with standard DoD or commercial practices.
             ii. Software Integration, Assembly, Test and Checkout
         M.7.1.5.3 Specialized Test Equipment. Data collection, safety and communication equipment
required to support a test.
        Includes for example:
        a. Safety Equipment/Assets
        b. Communications Equipment/Assets
        c. Telemetry/Tracking Equipment/Assets
        d. Optics Equipment/Assets
        e. Mission Support Activities 1…n (Specify). Includes all mission specific task to support test execution

        Excludes, for example:
        a. Sustainment activities to maintain mission readiness that are not allocated to a specific test.

         M.7.1.5.4 Commercial. Non-Government owned or operated test facilities. Note: Program offices
pay for the equipment and for usage.
        Includes, for example:
        a. Non-Government ranges/facilities used to test missile weapon System or missile subsystem.
        b. Non-Government laboratories, part of a building, or other place equipped to conduct scientific
            experiments or tests.
        c. Other Test Facilities. Non-Government owned or operated test facilities not listed.
        M.7.1.5.5 Government (Pay-as-You-Go). Government owned or operated test facilities. Note:
Program offices do not pay for the equipment but do need to pay for usage.
        Includes, for example:
        a. Ranges - Government facilities used to test missile weapon System or missile subsystem.
        b. Laboratories - A Government building, part of a building, or other place equipped to conduct
            scientific experiments or tests.
        c. Ranges/Facilities - use of the facility, including equipment brought or provided, “man-hours”, and
            “special test equipment.”
        d. Other Test Facilities - Government owned or operated test facilities not listed.

 M.8 Work Breakdown Structure Levels and Definitions for Government System Test and Evaluation

 WORK BREAKDOWN STRUCTURE LEVELS

 WBS #       Level 1 Level 2 Level 3 Level 4
 1.0         Sea System
 1.1                 Ship
 1.2                 Systems Engineering
 1.3                 Program Management
 1.4                 System Test and Evaluation
 1.4.1                         Developmental Test and Evaluation
 1.4.1.1                                  Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation
 1.4.1.2                                  Other DT&E Tests 1…n (Specify)
 1.4.2                         Operational Test and Evaluation
 1.4.2.1                                  Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation
 1.4.2.2                                  Other OT&E Tests 1…n (Specify)

 1.4.4                         Mock-ups/System Integration Labs (SILs)

 1.5                 Training
 1.6                 Data
 1.7                 Peculiar Support Equipment
 1.8                 Common Support Equipment
 1.9                 Operational/Site Activation by Site 1...n (Specify)
 1.10                Contractor Logistics Support (CLS)
 1.11                Industrial Facilities
 1.12                Initial Spares and Repair Parts

         M.8.1 System Test and Evaluation. The use of pilot, prototype, production, or specifically fabricated
hardware/software to obtain or validate engineering data on the performance of the system during the developmental
phase of the program. It also includes all effort associated with the development of any specialized tools or data in
support of the system level test program.
           Includes, for example:
           a. Detailed planning, conduct, support, data reduction and reports (excluding the contract data
               requirements list data) from such testing, and test articles that are functionally configured to represent
               and test subsystems/components of the defense materiel end item being developed or produced
           b. Design and production of models, specimens, fixtures and instrumentation
           c. Development and/or validation of modeling and simulation resources required for Test and Evaluation
               of a specific system under test.
           d. System level labs that integrate both hardware and software
           e. Training for Government personnel to provide knowledge and capability to deliver test and evaluation
               support actions as required.

NOTE: Test articles that are functionally configured to represent and test the complete defense
materiel end item being developed or produced, are excluded from this WBS element.

           Excludes, for example:
           a. All formal and informal testing up through the subsystem level, which can be associated with the
               hardware/software element acceptance testing


                                                          268

                             Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                           Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                 MIL-STD-881F
                                                 APPENDIX M

NOTE: These excluded efforts are to be included with the appropriate hardware or software elements.



          M.8.1.1 Developmental Test and Evaluation. Developmental Test & Evaluation (DT&E) is
conducted throughout the acquisition process to assist in engineering design and development and to verify
that technical performance specifications have been met. DT&E is planned and monitored by the
developing agency and is normally conducted by the contractor. It includes the T&E of components,
subsystems, Preplanned Product Improvement (P3I) changes, hardware/software integration, and
production qualification testing. It encompasses the use of models, simulations, test beds, and prototypes
or full-scale engineering development models of the system.

         Includes test and evaluation conducted to:
         a. Demonstrate that the engineering design and development process is complete
         b. Demonstrate that the design risks have been minimized
         c. Demonstrate that the system will meet specifications
         d. Assess the system’s military utility when introduced
         e. Determine whether the engineering design is supportable (practical, maintainable, safe, etc.) for
              operational use
         f. Provide test data with which to examine and evaluate trade-offs against specification requirements,
              life cycle cost, and schedule
         g. Perform the logistics testing efforts to evaluate the achievement of supportability goals, the
              adequacy of the support package for the system, (e.g., deliverable maintenance tools, test
              equipment, technical publications, maintenance instructions, and personnel skills and training
              requirements, etc.)

          M.8.1.1.1 Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation. The developmental test and evaluation (DT&E) conducted
and monitored by the developing agency of the DoD. This will determine the DT&E results for Low Rate Initial
Production and the Limited Deployment Phase associated with Cooperative Vulnerability Identification and
Adversarial Cybersecurity. See Figure XII for Cybersecurity Testing by phase requirements. Such activities include,
for example, developing cybersecurity test objectives, testing security standards, operational resilience, and system
cyber survivability. Also includes planning cybersecurity test infrastructure, adversarial DT&E, and test operational
resistance. The following DoD Guidebooks and Instruction (latest versions) should be followed for Cybersecurity
test and evaluation planning; in addition to specific Service-related guidebooks or instructions, as appropriate.

         a.   DoD Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation Guidebook
         b.   Army Regulation 73-1 Test and Evaluation Policy
         c.   Director, Operational Test and Evaluation (DOT&E), Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP)
              Guidebook,
         d.   DoD Instruction, No. 8510.01; “Risk Management Framework (RMF) for DoD Information
              Technology”
         e.   DoD Instruction 5000.83 “Technology and Program Protection to Maintain Technological
              Advantage”
         f.   DoD Instruction 5000.90 “Cybersecurity and Acquisition Decision Authorities and Program
              Managers”

         M.8.1.1.2 Service/Agency DT&E 1...n (Specify). The costs to rent, lease, procure, or utilize a military
weapon system in order to support the testing of a different, primary weapon system. These costs are normally
paid to by the primary weapon system program being tested to another program office/program in order to utilize
equipment or systems needed to test or characterize the primary weapon system.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Costs to utilize a ship, aircraft, ground vehicle used to act as an opposing force (e.g., “red team”).
         b. Costs to repair or demilitarize a non-primary military system damaged during the testing of the
              primary weapon system.

         Excludes, for example:

          M.8.1.2 Operational Test and Evaluation. The test and evaluation conducted by agencies other than the
developing command to assess the prospective system’s military utility, operational effectiveness, operational
suitability, logistics supportability (including compatibility, inter-operability, reliability, maintainability, logistic
requirements, etc.) The system is also being tested against the operational doctrine suitable for the system against
the threats expected to be encountered by the system, cost of ownership, and need for any modifications.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Initial operational test and evaluation conducted during the development of a system
         b. Such tests as system demonstration, flight tests, sea trials, mobility demonstrations, on-orbit tests,
             spin demonstration, stability tests, qualification operational test and evaluation, etc., and support
             thereto, required to prove the operational capability of the deliverable system.
         c. Contractor support (e.g., technical assistance, maintenance, labor, material, etc.) consumed during
             this phase of testing
         d. Logistics testing efforts to evaluate the achievement of supportability goals and the adequacy of
             the support for the system (e.g., deliverable maintenance tools, test equipment, technical
             publications, maintenance instructions, personnel skills and training requirements, and software
             support facility/environment elements).
         e. Pre-full-rate production/full deployment tests - Common to all systems, including (1) Early User Test
             (EUT). (2) Limited User Test (LUT). (3) Initial Operating Test (IOT).
         f. Post-full-rate production/full deployment tests, including: (1) Full Operational Test (FOT). (2) User
             acceptance test (UAT) and unique to IT systems test.

         g.   As-required tests, including (1) Customer test (CT) (common to all systems). (2) Supplemental site
