           M.8.1.2.1 Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation. The operational test and evaluation (OT&E) conducted and
monitored by DoD agencies. The technical efforts of ensuring the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of
information and technology by monitoring and defending against cyber (network) attacks. Early operational testing
(that is, EUT and LUT), as well as Initial Operation and Test (IOT) will determine the OT&E results for Vulnerability
and Penetration Assessment and Adversarial Assessment in Full Rate Production or Full Deployment. See Figure
XII for Cybersecurity Testing by phase requirements. Such activities include, for example, identifying mission-
impacting deficiencies and resolutions, completing residual DT&E actions, performing operational test readiness,
remediation of all missions impacting deficiencies and verification, validation of accreditation for all ranges,
simulations to be involved in the event. The following DoD Guidebooks and Instruction (latest versions) should be
followed for Cybersecurity test and evaluation planning; in addition to specific Service-related guidebooks or
instructions, as appropriate.

         a.   DoD Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation Guidebook
         b.   Director, Operational Test and Evaluation (DOT&E), Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP)
              Guidebook,
         c.   DoD Instruction, No. 8510.01; “Risk Management Framework (RMF) for DoD Information
              Technology”
         d.   DoD Instruction 5000.83 “Technology and Program Protection to Maintain Technological
              Advantage”
         e.   DoD Instruction 5000.90 “Cybersecurity and Acquisition Decision Authorities and Program
              Managers”

         M.8.1.2.2 Other OT&E Tests 1…n (Specify) Other OT&E tests not captured elsewhere in this
commodity. The following are examples of OT&E tests that may be applicable to this commodity but were
not identified. If pertinent, include those that may be appropriate, by specifically listing them under this WBS
element (M.8.2.2) NOTE: Each test should be identified by the next consecutive WBS number (e.g., M.8.2.2.1,
M.8.2.2.2, etc.)

         a.   Production Verification Test (PVT) and Evaluation
         b.   First Article Test (FAT) and Evaluation
         c.   Comparison test
         d.   Quality Conformance Inspection
         e.   Lot acceptance test
         f.   Post-Deployment Software Support (PDSS) Test
         g.   Surveillance Test
         h.   Reconditioning Test (Pilot or Initial)
         i.   Control Test
         j.   Acceptance Test
         k.   Baseline Evaluation Test
         l.   Regression Test (unique to software-intensive systems.)
          M.8.1.3 Live Fire Test and Evaluation. The test and evaluation managed by the Program Office and
conducted by dedicated Service laboratories and commands to assess the system’s survivability or lethality
against all operationally representative and relevant threats or targets. It includes test and evaluation conducted
early to identify and mitigate system design vulnerabilities that could affect the mission or force protection
capabilities. It also progresses through OT&E to verify any design changes and to assess the survivability of a
production representative system prior to fielding or full-rate production (FRP). LFT&E includes component,
sub-system, and system-level testing as well as M&S, and evaluation of combat data, safety data, mishap data,
and system designs to:

         a.   Characterize and improve the ability of the system to avoid being hit during its mission. Examples
              include assessment of system signatures, active protection systems or self-defense systems intended
              to mitigate hit probabilities or manipulate hit distributions,

                                                         271

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                 MIL-STD-881F
                                                 APPENDIX M

         b.   Identify and mitigate threat induced system design vulnerabilities to improve residual mission
              capability post attack,
         c.   Characterize threat induced crew casualties to identify and implement system design changes that
              could improve force protection in combat,
         d.   Identify and improve system design changes and procedures that could advance system
              recoverability post-combat engagement.
         M.8.1.4 Mock-ups/System Integration Labs (SILs). The design engineering and production of system
or subsystem mock-ups that have special contractual or engineering significance or that are not required solely
for the conduct of one of the above elements of testing. SILs are often used in lieu of (or in addition to) mock-
ups. SILs are risk reduction facilities where software and hardware can be developed, integrated, tested, and
evaluated for both standalone functionality and/or interoperability prior to being fielded.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Hardware/lab equipment
         b. SIL Software (written to simulate the operating environment or written to operate the SIL)
         M.8.1.5 Test and Evaluation Support. The support elements necessary to operate and maintain, during
test and evaluation, systems and subsystems, which are not consumed during the testing phase and are not
allocated to a specific phase of testing.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Repairable spares, repair of reparables, repair parts, consumables, warehousing and distribution of
             spares and repair parts, test and support equipment, test bed vehicles, drones, surveillance
             aircraft/chase aircraft, tracking vessels, contractor technical support
         Excludes, for example:
         a. Operational and maintenance personnel, special fixtures, special instrumentation, etc., that are
             utilized and/or consumed in a single element of testing and that should be included under that element
             of testing
          M.8.1.6 Test Facilities. The special test facilities required for performance of the various developmental
tests necessary to prove the design and reliability of the system or subsystem.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Test tank test fixtures, propulsion test fixtures, white rooms, test chambers, range/targeting facilities
         b. Cost to the program of developing and/or providing the test capability.
         Excludes, for example:
         a. Brick and mortar-type facilities identified as industrial facilities
         b. Cost to the program for using the capability is not included in this WBS. The cost of providing this
             effort is associated with the WBS it supports (i.e., the actual testing effort).
         M.8.1.6.1 Commercial. Non-Government owned or operated test facilities. Note: Program offices
do pay for the equipment and for usage.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Non-Government ranges/facilities used to test missile weapon System or missile subsystem.
         b. Non-Government laboratories, part of a building, or other place equipped to conduct scientific
             experiments or tests.
         c. Other Test Facilities. Non-Government owned or operated test facilities not listed.
        M.8.1.6.2 Government (Pay-as-You-Go). Government owned or operated test facilities. Note:
Program offices do not pay for the equipment but do need to pay for usage.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Ranges - Government facilities used to test missile weapon System or missile subsystem.
         b. Laboratories - A Government building, part of a building, or other place equipped to conduct
              scientific experiments or tests.
         c. Ranges/Facilities - use of the facility, including equipment brought or provided, “man-hours”, and

                         Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                         MIL-STD-881F
                                         APPENDIX M

     “special test equipment.”
d.   Other Test Facilities - Government owned or operated test facilities not listed.

                                                  MIL-STD-881F
                                                  APPENDIX M

         M.9.1 System Test and Evaluation (ST&E). The use of pilot, prototype, production, or specifically
fabricated hardware/software to obtain or validate engineering data on the performance of the system during
the developmental phase of the program. It also includes all effort associated with the development of any
specialized tools or data in support of the system level test program.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Development of test plans and procedures
         b. Test preparation, test conduct, test support, test management, and data reduction and reports
             (excluding contract data requirements list data) from such testing, and test articles that are configured
             to represent and test subsystems/components of the defense materiel end item being developed or
             produced
         c. Design and production of models, test units, and test equipment, and
         d. Development and/or validation of modeling and simulation resources required for system test and
             evaluation of a specific system under test.

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Integration and test efforts that can be associated specifically with the equipment (hardware/software)
             for a unit or CSCI (Level 5 element).
         b. All formal and informal testing up through the subsystem level, which can be associated with the
             hardware/software element acceptance testing

          M.9.1.1 Developmental Test and Evaluation. Developmental Test & Evaluation (DT&E) is
conducted throughout the acquisition process to assist in engineering design and development and to verify
that technical performance specifications have been met. DT&E is planned and monitored by the
developing agency and is normally conducted by the contractor. It includes the T&E of components,
subsystems, Preplanned Product Improvement (P3I) changes, hardware/software integration, and
production qualification testing. It encompasses the use of models, simulations, test beds, and prototypes
or full-scale engineering development models of the system.

         Includes test and evaluation conducted to:
         a. Demonstrate that the engineering design and development process is complete
         b. Demonstrate that the design risks have been minimized
         c. Demonstrate that the system will meet specifications
         d. Assess the system’s military utility when introduced
         e. Determine whether the engineering design is supportable (practical, maintainable, safe, etc.) for
              operational use
         f. Provide test data with which to examine and evaluate trade-offs against specification requirements,
              life cycle cost, and schedule

         M.9.1.1.1 Functional/Performance Developmental Test and Evaluation. Developmental testing and
evaluation to understand the function and performance of a test article in order to demonstrate compliance with
specifications, to inform design trades, to identify and address program risk, and similar activities on a segment
(space, ground, user, or launch, as applicable) or an integrated system. At the integrated system level, tests are not
to evaluate the proper integration of the system, but how the system as implemented functions in a representative
environment as compared to specifications. Test articles evaluated may be production-representative or
prototype/engineering units.

          M.9.1.1.2 Cybersecurity System Test and Evaluation. The developmental test and evaluation
conducted and monitored by the developing agency of the DoD. The technical efforts of ensuring the
confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information and technology by monitoring and defending
against cyber (network) attacks. This also includes RMF assessment activities that support information
system approval to operate. The following DoD Guidebooks and Instruction (latest versions) should be
followed for Cybersecurity test and evaluation planning; in addition to specific Service-related guidebooks
or instructions, as appropriate.

         a.   DoD Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation Guidebook, Version 2.0 particularly CTTs, CVIs, ACD, etc.,

                                                         275

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                  MIL-STD-881F
                                                  APPENDIX M

         b.   Director, Operational Test and Evaluation (DOT&E), Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP)
              Guidebook,
         c.   DoD Instruction, No. 8510.01; “Risk Management Framework (RMF) for DoD Information
              Technology (IT)”

          M.9.1.1.3 Survivability Test and Evaluation. The test and evaluation managed by or on behalf of the
Program Office and conducted by dedicated Service laboratories and commands to assess the system’s
survivability against operationally representative and relevant threats. It includes test and evaluation conducted
early to identify and mitigate system design vulnerabilities that could affect the mission or force protection
capabilities. It also progresses through OT&E to verify any design changes and to assess the survivability of a
production representative system prior to fielding or further production. Survivability includes component, sub-
system, and system-level testing as well as M&S, and evaluation of fielded system data, mishap/anomaly data, and
system designs which:

         Includes, for example:
         a. Characterize and improve the ability of the system to avoid being hit during its mission. Examples
             include assessment of system signatures, active protection systems or self-defense systems intended
             to mitigate hit probabilities, or other measures.
         b. Identify and mitigate threat induced system design vulnerabilities to improve residual mission
             capability post attack,
         c. Characterize ground system vulnerabilities to identify and implement system design changes to
             improve force protection,
         d. Identify and improve system design changes and procedures that could advance system recoverability
             post-combat engagement.

         M.9.1.1.4 Modeling and Simulation (M&S). Development, verification, and validation of computer
models and the simulations to run them. M&S is used to provide additional insight into system performance where
prohibited by safety, policy, or other resource constraints, and includes the collection of real-world data specifically
for the purpose of ensuring the model appropriately reflects reality.

         M.9.1.1.5 System Checkout and Calibration. Activities intended to verify adherence to performance and
other specifications upon deployment of the system into its intended environment, and typically post launch for
spacecraft. A small subset of activities, particularly those related to ground system deployment may precede
launch. Events described under this heading are accomplished at the segment (ground, launch segment, space
segment) level. Calibration events are those where system parameters are adjusted to meet desired performance.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Mission data processing throughput testing
         b. Payload tuning to set appropriate sensitivity parameters for the mission set
         c. Maneuvers to characterize on-orbit propulsion system performance
         d. Adjustment of control system gains to improve payload jitter performance

         M.9.1.1.6 Service/Agency DT&E 1...n (Specify). The costs to rent, lease, procure, or utilize a military
weapon system in order to support the testing of a different, primary weapon system. These costs are normally
paid to by the primary weapon system program being tested to another program office/program in order to utilize
equipment or systems needed to test or characterize the primary weapon system.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Costs to utilize a ship, aircraft, ground vehicle used to act as an opposing force (e.g., “red team”).
         b. Costs to repair or demilitarize a non-primary military system damaged during the testing of the
             primary weapon system.

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Costs to procure, repair, or demilitarize the primary weapon system being tested.

         M.9.1.1.7 Other DT&E Tests 1…n (Specify). Other DT&E tests not captured elsewhere in this

                                                         276

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                   MIL-STD-881F
                                                   APPENDIX M

commodity. The following are examples of DT&E tests that may be applicable to this commodity but were
not identified. If pertinent, include those that may be appropriate, by specifically listing them by starting with
this WBS element (M.9.1.7) NOTE: Each test should be identified by the next consecutive WBS number (e.g.,
M.9.1.7.1, M.9.1.7.2, etc.).
          M.9.1.2 Operational Test and Evaluation. The test and evaluation conducted by agencies other than the
developing command to assess the prospective system’s military utility, operational effectiveness, operational
suitability, logistics supportability (including compatibility, inter-operability, reliability, maintainability, logistic
requirements, etc.) cost of ownership, and need for any modifications.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Early Operational Assessments (EOA) and Operational Assessments (OA) conducted to identify and
             reduce risk to the program,
         b. Initial operational test and evaluation conducted during the development of a system,
         c. Such tests as system demonstration, flight tests, sea trials, mobility demonstrations, on-orbit tests,
             capability demonstrations, qualification operational test and evaluation, etc., and support thereto,
             required to prove the operational capability of the deliverable system.
         d. Contractor support (e.g., technical assistance, maintenance, labor, material, etc.) consumed during
             this phase of testing

         M.9.1.2.1 Functional/Performance Operational Test and Evaluation. Operational test conducted for the
purposes of establishing proper function and operational performance of the system, to include space, ground, and
user segments (and launch, as applicable) to meet end-user needs. This includes the OT&E of individuals segments
of the space system, system level testing, and system of systems testing, early assessments, initial OT&E, and any
post-acceptance operational testing.
         M.9.1.2.2 Interoperability Testing. The process of assessing the ability of a system to exchange usable
electronic information with systems of other services or nations as specified in its requirements documents.
          M.9.1.2.3 Cybersecurity System Test and Evaluation. The operational test and evaluation
conducted and monitored by the developing agency of the DoD. The technical efforts of ensuring the
confidentiality, integrity, and availability of information and technology by monitoring and defending
against cyber (network) attacks. The following DoD Guidebooks and Instruction (latest versions) should be
followed for Cybersecurity test and evaluation planning; in addition to specific Service-related guidebooks
or instructions, as appropriate.
         a.   DoD Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation Guidebook, Version 2.0, particularly CVPAs and
              Adversarial Assessments.
          M.9.1.2.4 Survivability Test and Evaluation. The test and evaluation managed by or on behalf of the
Program Office and conducted by dedicated Service laboratories and commands to assess the system’s
survivability against operationally representative and relevant threats. It includes test and evaluation conducted
early to identify and mitigate system design vulnerabilities that could affect the mission or force protection
capabilities. It also progresses through OT&E to verify any design changes and to assess the survivability of a
production representative system prior to further production. Survivability includes component, sub-system, and
system-level testing as well as survivability-related M&S, and evaluation of fielded system data, mishap/anomaly
data, and system designs to:
         a.   Characterize and improve the ability of the system to avoid being hit during its mission. Examples
              include assessment of system signatures, active protection systems or self-defense systems intended
              to mitigate hit probabilities, or other measures.
         b.   Identify and mitigate threat induced system design vulnerabilities to improve residual mission
              capability post attack,
         c.   Characterize ground system vulnerabilities to identify and implement system design changes to
              improve force protection,
         d.   Identify and improve system design changes and procedures that could advance system recoverability
              post-combat engagement.

          M.9.1.2.5 Modeling and Simulation (M&S). Development, verification, validation, and certification of
computer models and the simulations to run them. M&S is used to provide additional insight into system
performance where prohibited by safety, policy, or other resource constraints, and includes the collection of real-
world data specifically for the purpose of ensuring the model appropriately reflects reality. Models used for normal
and contingency operations are accredited or certified by OT&E organizations.

           M.9.1.2.6 Cooperative Vulnerability and Penetration Assessment (CVPA). This test examines the system
to identify vulnerabilities by using data from cooperative cybersecurity test events to characterize the cybersecurity
and operational resilience of the system in an operational context and provide reconnaissance of the system.

         M.9.1.2.7 RMF Security Controls Assessment Testing done to assess implementation and
 compliance with RMF controls by assessing RMF artifacts, conduct security control assessments, etc.

           M.9.1.2.8 Other OT&E Tests 1…n (Specify) Other OT&E tests not captured elsewhere in this
 commodity. If there are examples of OT&E tests that may be applicable to this commodity but were not
 identified. If pertinent, include those that may be appropriate, by specifically listing them under this WBS
 element (M.9.2.8) NOTE: Each test should be identified by the next consecutive WBS number (e.g., M.9.2.8.1,
 M.9.2.8.2, etc.)
           .
           M.9.1.3 Mock-ups/System Integration Labs (SILs). The design engineering and production of system
 or subsystem mock-ups that have special contractual or engineering significance or that are not required solely
 for the conduct of one of the above elements of testing. SILs are often used in lieu of (or in addition to) mock-
 ups. SILs are risk reduction facilities where software and hardware can be developed, integrated, tested, and
 evaluated for both standalone functionality and/or interoperability prior to being fielded.

          Includes, for example:
          a. Hardware/lab equipment
          b. SIL Software (written to simulate the operating environment or written to operate the SIL)

          M.9.1.4 Test and Evaluation Support. The support elements necessary to operate and maintain, during
 test and evaluation, systems and subsystems, which are not consumed during the testing phase and are not
 allocated to a specific phase of testing.

          Includes, for example:
          a. Repairable spares, repair of reparables, repair parts, consumables, warehousing and distribution of
             spares and repair parts, test and support equipment, test bed vehicles, drones, surveillance
             aircraft/chase aircraft, tracking vessels, contractor technical support

          Excludes, for example:
          a. Operational and maintenance personnel, special fixtures, special instrumentation, etc., that are utilized
             and/or consumed in a single element of testing and that should be included under that element of
             testing

          M.9.1.5 Test Facilities. The special test facilities required for performance of the various developmental
 tests necessary to prove the design and reliability of the system or subsystem.

          Includes, for example:
          a. Test tank test fixtures, propulsion test fixtures, white rooms, test chambers, range/targeting facilities
          b. Cost to the program of developing and/or providing the test capability.

          Excludes, for example:
          a. Brick and mortar-type facilities identified as industrial facilities
          b. Cost to the program for using the capability is not included in this WBS. The cost of providing this
              effort is associated with the WBS it supports (i.e., the actual testing effort).

          M.10.1 System Test and Evaluation. The use of pilot, prototype, production, or specifically fabricated
 hardware/software to obtain or validate engineering data on the performance of the system during the developmental
 phase of the program. It also includes all effort associated with the development of any specialized tools or data in
 support of the system level test program.

          Includes, for example:
          a. Detailed planning, conduct, support, data reduction and reports (excluding the contract data
              requirements list data) from such testing, and test articles that are functionally configured to represent
              and test subsystems/components of the defense materiel end item being developed or produced
          b. Design and production of models, specimens, fixtures and instrumentation
          c. Development and/or validation of modeling and simulation resources required for Test and Evaluation
              of a specific system under test.
          d. System level labs that integrate both hardware and software
          e. Training for Government personnel to provide knowledge and capability to deliver test and evaluation
              support actions as required.

         Excludes, for example:
         a. All formal and informal testing up through the subsystem level, which can be associated with the
             hardware/software element acceptance testing

NOTE: These excluded efforts are to be included with the appropriate hardware or software elements.


         M.10.1.1 Developmental Test and Evaluation. Developmental Test & Evaluation (DT&E) is conducted
throughout the acquisition process to assist in engineering design and development and to verify that technical
performance specifications have been met. DT&E is planned and monitored by the developing agency and is normally
conducted by the contractor. It includes the T&E of components, subsystems, Preplanned Product Improvement (P3I)
changes, hardware/software integration, and production qualification testing. It encompasses the use of models,
simulations, test beds, and prototypes or full-scale engineering development models of the system.

         Includes test and evaluation conducted to:
         a. Demonstrate that the engineering design and development process is complete
         b. Demonstrate that the design risks have been minimized
         c. Demonstrate that the system will meet specifications
         d. Assess the system’s military utility when introduced
         e. Determine whether the engineering design is supportable (practical, maintainable, safe, etc.) for
              operational use
         f. Provide test data with which to examine and evaluate trade-offs against specification requirements, life
              cycle cost, and schedule
         g. Perform the logistics testing efforts to evaluate the achievement of supportability goals, the adequacy of
              the support package for the system, (e.g., deliverable maintenance tools, test equipment, technical
              publications, maintenance instructions, and personnel skills and training requirements, etc.)

         M.10.1.1.1 Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation. The developmental test and evaluation (DT&E) conducted
and monitored by the developing agency of the DoD. This will determine the DT&E results for Low Rate Initial
Production and the Limited Deployment Phase associated with Cooperative Vulnerability Identification and
Adversarial Cybersecurity. See Figure XII for Cybersecurity Testing by phase requirements. Such activities include,
for example, developing cybersecurity test objectives, testing security standards, operational resilience, and system
cyber survivability. Also includes planning cybersecurity test infrastructure, adversarial DT&E, and test operational
resistance. The following DoD Guidebooks and Instruction (latest versions) should be followed for Cybersecurity test
and evaluation planning; in addition to specific Service-related guidebooks or instructions, as appropriate.

         a.   DoD Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation Guidebook
         b.   Army Regulation 73-1 Test and Evaluation Policy
         c.   DoD Instruction, No. 8510.01; “Risk Management Framework (RMF) for DoD Information
              Technology”
         d.   DoD Instruction 5000.83 “Technology and Program Protection to Maintain Technological Advantage”
         e.   DoD Instruction 5000.90 “Cybersecurity and Acquisition Decision Authorities and Program Managers”

         M.10.1.1.2 Service/Agency DT&E 1...n (Specify). The costs to rent, lease, procure, or utilize a military
weapon system in order to support the testing of a different, primary weapon system. These costs are normally
paid to by the primary weapon system program being tested to another program office/program in order to utilize
equipment or systems needed to test or characterize the primary weapon system.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Costs to utilize a ship, aircraft, ground vehicle used to act as an opposing force (e.g., “red team”).
         b. Costs to repair or demilitarize a non-primary military system damaged during the testing of the
             primary weapon system.

                           Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                         Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                  MIL-STD-881F
                                                  APPENDIX M


         Excludes, for example:
         a. Costs to procure, repair, or demilitarize the primary weapon system being tested.

         M.10.1.1.3 Other DT&E Tests 1…n (Specify). Other DT&E tests not captured elsewhere in this commodity.
The following are examples of DT&E tests that may be applicable to this commodity but were not identified. If
pertinent, include those that may be appropriate, by specifically listing them by starting with this WBS element
(M.10.1.3) NOTE: Each test should be identified by the next consecutive WBS number (e.g., M.10.1.3.1, M.10.1.3.2,
etc.).


         Examples of Developmental Tests
         a. Market Research effort (or proof of concept test) and Evaluation
         b. Technical Feasibility Test (TFT) and Evaluation
         c. Engineering development test (EDT) and Evaluation
         d. Production prove-out test (PPT) and Evaluation
         e. Production qualification Test (PQT) and Evaluation
         f. Software development test (SDT) and Evaluation
         g. Software qualification test (SQT) and Evaluation
         h. Interoperability certification test and Evaluation
         i. Soldier protection testing and Evaluation
         j. Technical Manual (TM) Verification
         k. Physical configuration audit (PCA)/Functional configuration audit (FCA)

         Examples of Pre-Operational Tests
         a. Production Verification Test (PVT) and Evaluation
         b. First Article Test (FAT) and Evaluation
         c. Comparison test
         d. Quality Conformance Inspection
         e. Lot acceptance test
         f. Post-Deployment Software Support (PDSS) Test
         g. Surveillance Test
         h. Reconditioning Test (Pilot or Initial)
         i. Control Test
         j. Acceptance Test
         k. Baseline Evaluation Test

          M.10.1.2 Operational Test and Evaluation. The test and evaluation conducted by agencies other than the
developing command to assess the prospective system’s military utility, operational effectiveness, operational
suitability, logistics supportability (including compatibility, inter-operability, reliability, maintainability, logistic
requirements, etc.) The system is also being tested against the operational doctrine suitable for the system against the
threats expected to be encountered by the system, cost of ownership, and need for any modifications.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Initial operational test and evaluation conducted during the development of a system
         b. Such tests as system demonstration, flight tests, sea trials, mobility demonstrations, on-orbit tests, spin
             demonstration, stability tests, qualification operational test and evaluation, etc., and support thereto,
             required to prove the operational capability of the deliverable system.
         c. Contractor support (e.g., technical assistance, maintenance, labor, material, etc.) consumed during this
             phase of testing
         d. Logistics testing efforts to evaluate the achievement of supportability goals and the adequacy of the
             support for the system (e.g., deliverable maintenance tools, test equipment, technical publications,
             maintenance instructions, personnel skills and training requirements, and software support
             facility/environment elements).
         e. Pre-full-rate production/full deployment tests - Common to all systems, including (1) Early User Test
             (EUT). (2) Limited User Test (LUT). (3) Initial Operating Test (IOT).

                                                         282

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                  MIL-STD-881F
                                                  APPENDIX M

         f.   Post-full-rate production/full deployment tests, including: (1) Full Operational Test (FOT). (2) User
              acceptance test (UAT) and unique to IT systems test.
         g.   As-required tests, including (1) Customer test (CT) (common to all systems). (2) Supplemental site test
              (SST) (unique to IT systems). (3) Regression test (unique to software-intensive systems).

           M.10.1.2.1 Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation. The operational test and evaluation (OT&E) conducted and
monitored by DoD agencies. The technical efforts of ensuring the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of
information and technology by monitoring and defending against cyber (network) attacks. Early operational testing
(that is, EUT and LUT), as well as Initial Operation and Test (IOT) will determine the OT&E results for Vulnerability
and Penetration Assessment and Adversarial Assessment in Full Rate Production or Full Deployment. See Figure XII
for Cybersecurity Testing by phase requirements. Such activities include, for example, identifying mission-impacting
deficiencies and resolutions, completing residual DT&E actions, performing operational test readiness, remediation
of all missions impacting deficiencies and verification, validation of accreditation for all ranges, simulations to be
involved in the event. The following DoD Guidebooks and Instruction (latest versions) should be followed for
Cybersecurity test and evaluation planning; in addition to specific Service-related guidebooks or instructions, as
appropriate.

         a.   DoD Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation Guidebook, Version 2.0
         b.   Army Regulation 73-1 Test and Evaluation Policy
         c.   Director, Operational Test and Evaluation (DOT&E), Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP)
              Guidebook,
         d.   DoD Instruction, No. 8510.01; “Risk Management Framework (RMF) for DoD Information Technology
         e.   DoD Instruction 5000.83 “Technology and Program Protection to Maintain Technological Advantage”
         f.   DoD Instruction 5000.90 “Cybersecurity and Acquisition Decision Authorities and Program Managers”

         M.10.1.2.2 Other OT&E Tests 1…n (Specify). Other OT&E tests not captured elsewhere in this commodity.
The following are examples of OT&E tests that may be applicable to this commodity but were not identified. If
pertinent, include those that may be appropriate, by specifically listing them under this WBS element (M.10.2.2)
NOTE: Each test should be identified by the next consecutive WBS number (e.g., M.10.2.2.1, M.10.2.2.2, etc.)

         a.   Production Verification Test (PVT) and Evaluation
         b.   First Article Test (FAT) and Evaluation
         c.   Comparison test
         d.   Quality Conformance Inspection
         e.   Lot acceptance test
         f.   Post-Deployment Software Support (PDSS) Test
         g.   Surveillance Test
         h.   Reconditioning Test (Pilot or Initial)
         i.   Control Test
         j.   Acceptance Test
         k.   Baseline Evaluation Test
         l.   Regression Test (unique to software-intensive systems.

          M.10.1.3 Live Fire Test and Evaluation. The test and evaluation managed by the Program Office and
conducted by dedicated Service laboratories and commands to assess the system’s survivability or lethality against all
operationally representative and relevant threats or targets. It includes test and evaluation conducted early to identify
and mitigate system design vulnerabilities that could affect the mission or force protection capabilities. It also
progresses through OT&E to verify any design changes and to assess the survivability of a production representative
system prior to fielding or full-rate production (FRP). LFT&E includes component, sub-system, and system-level
testing as well as M&S, and evaluation of combat data, safety data, mishap data, and system designs to:
          a. Characterize and improve the ability of the system to avoid being hit during its mission. Examples
              include assessment of system signatures, active protection systems or self-defense systems intended to
              mitigate hit probabilities or manipulate hit distributions,
          b. Identify and mitigate threat induced system design vulnerabilities to improve residual mission capability
              post attack,
          c. Characterize threat induced crew casualties to identify and implement system design changes that could
              improve force protection in combat,

                                                         283

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                 MIL-STD-881F
                                                 APPENDIX M

         d. Identify and improve system design changes and procedures that could advance system recoverability
            post-combat engagement.

         M.10.1.4 Mock-ups/System Integration Labs (SILs). The design engineering and production of system or
subsystem mock-ups that have special contractual or engineering significance or that are not required solely for the
conduct of one of the above elements of testing. SILs are often used in lieu of (or in addition to) mock-ups. SILs are
risk reduction facilities where software and hardware can be developed, integrated, tested, and evaluated for both
standalone functionality and/or interoperability prior to being fielded.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Hardware/lab equipment
         b. SIL Software (written to simulate the operating environment or written to operate the SIL)

         M.10.1.5 Test and Evaluation Support. The support elements necessary to operate and maintain, during test
and evaluation, systems and subsystems, which are not consumed during the testing phase and are not allocated to a
specific phase of testing.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Repairable spares, repair of reparables, repair parts, consumables, warehousing and distribution of
              spares and repair parts, test and support equipment, test bed vehicles, drones, surveillance aircraft/chase
              aircraft, tracking vessels, contractor technical support

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Operational and maintenance personnel, special fixtures, special instrumentation, etc., that are utilized
             and/or consumed in a single element of testing and that should be included under that element of testing.

         M.10.1.6 Test Facilities. The special test facilities required for performance of the various developmental
tests necessary to prove the design and reliability of the system or subsystem.

         Includes, for example:
         a.   Test tank test fixtures, propulsion test fixtures, white rooms, test chambers, range/targeting facilities
         b. Cost to the program of developing and/or providing the test capability.

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Brick and mortar-type facilities identified as industrial facilities
         b. Cost to the program for using the capability is not included in this WBS. The cost of providing this effort
             is associated with the WBS it supports (i.e., the actual testing effort).
         M.10.1.6.1 Commercial. Non-Government owned or operated test facilities. Note: Program offices
pay for the equipment and for usage.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Non-Government ranges/facilities used to test missile weapon System or missile subsystem.
         b. Non-Government laboratories, part of a building, or other place equipped to conduct scientific
             experiments or tests.
         c. Other Test Facilities. Non-Government owned or operated test facilities not listed.
         M.10.1.6.2 Government (Pay-as-You-Go). Government owned or operated test facilities. Note: Program
offices do not pay for the equipment but do need to pay for usage.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Ranges - Government facilities used to test missile weapon System or missile subsystem.
         b. Laboratories - A Government building, part of a building, or other place equipped to conduct
              scientific experiments or tests.
         c. Ranges/Facilities - use of the facility, including equipment brought or provided, “man-hours,” and
              “special test equipment.”
         d. Other Test Facilities - Government owned or operated test facilities not listed.

          M.11.1 System Test and Evaluation. The use of pilot, prototype, production, or specifically fabricated
hardware/software to obtain or validate engineering data on the performance of the system during the
developmental phase of the program. It also includes all effort associated with the development of any specialized
tools or data in support of the system level test program.

           Includes, for example:
           a. Detailed planning, conduct, support, data reduction and reports (excluding the contract data
               requirements list data) from such testing, and test articles that are functionally configured to
               represent and test subsystems/components of the defense materiel end item being developed
               or produced
           b. Design and production of models, specimens, fixtures and instrumentation
           c. Development and/or validation of modeling and simulation resources required for Test and
               Evaluation of a specific system under test.
           d. System level labs that integrate both hardware and software
           e. Training for Government personnel to provide knowledge and capability to deliver test and
               evaluation support actions as required.

                                                 MIL-STD-881F

NOTE: Test articles that are functionally configured to represent and test the complete defense
materiel end item being developed or produced, are excluded from this WBS element.

        Excludes, for example:
        a. All formal and informal testing up through the subsystem level, which can be associated with the
            hardware/software element acceptance testing

NOTE: These excluded efforts are to be included with the appropriate hardware or software elements.


         M.11.1.1 Developmental Test and Evaluation. Developmental Test & Evaluation (DT&E) is conducted
throughout the acquisition process to assist in engineering design and development and to verify that technical
performance specifications have been met. DT&E is planned and monitored by the developing agency and is
normally conducted by the contractor. It includes the T&E of components, subsystems, Preplanned Product
Improvement (P3I) changes, hardware/software integration, and production qualification testing. It encompasses
the use of models, simulations, test beds, and prototypes or full-scale engineering development models of the
system.

         Includes test and evaluation conducted to:
         a. Demonstrate that the engineering design and development process is complete
         b. Demonstrate that the design risks have been minimized
         c. Demonstrate that the system will meet specifications
         d. Assess the system’s military utility when introduced
         e. Determine whether the engineering design is supportable (practical, maintainable, safe, etc.) for
              operational use
         f. Provide test data with which to examine and evaluate trade-offs against specification requirements,
              life cycle cost, and schedule
         g. Perform the logistics testing efforts to evaluate the achievement of supportability goals, the adequacy
              of the support package for the system, (e.g., deliverable maintenance tools, test equipment, technical
              publications, maintenance instructions, and personnel skills and training requirements, etc.)

          M.11.1.1.1 Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation. The developmental test and evaluation conducted
and monitored by the developing agency of the DoD. The technical efforts of ensuring the confidentiality,
integrity, and availability of information and technology by monitoring and defending against cyber
(network) attacks. The following DoD Guidebooks and Instruction (latest versions) should be followed for
Cybersecurity test and evaluation planning; in addition to specific Service-related guidebooks or
instructions, as appropriate.

        a.   DoD Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation Guidebook, Version 2.0
        b.   Director, Operational Test and Evaluation (DOT&E), Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP)
             Guidebook,
        c.   DoD Instruction, No. 8510.01. “Risk Management Framework (RMF) for DoD Information
             Technology (IT)”

         M.11.1.1.2 Service/Agency DT&E 1...n (Specify). The costs to rent, lease, procure, or utilize a military
weapon system in order to support the testing of a different, primary weapon system. These costs are normally
paid to by the primary weapon system program being tested to another program office/program in order to utilize
equipment or systems needed to test or characterize the primary weapon system.

        Includes, for example:
        a. Costs to utilize a ship, aircraft, ground vehicle used to act as an opposing force (e.g., “red team”).
        b. Costs to repair or demilitarize a non-primary military system damaged during the testing of the
            primary weapon system.

        Excludes, for example:

         d. Other Test Facilities - Government owned or operated test facilities not listed.

                                         LAUNCH VEHICLE SYSTEMS

M.12 Work Breakdown Structure Levels and Definitions for Government System Test and Evaluation

WORK BREAKDOWN STRUCTURE LEVELS

WBS #       Level 1 Level 2 Level 3 Level 4
1.0         Launch Vehicle System
1.1                 Systems Engineering, Integration, Test, Program Management (SEIT/PM)
1.1.1                         Systems Engineering (SE)
1.1.2                         Assembly, Integration and Test
1.1.3                         Program Management (PM)
1.2                 Launch Vehicle
1.3                 Mission Integration and Analysis 1...f (Specify)
1.4                 Launch Operations Site 1...n (Specify)
1.5                 Launch Site 1...n (Specify)
1.6                 System Test and Evaluation
1.6.1                         Developmental Test and Evaluation
1.6.1.1                                  Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation
1.6.1.2                                  Other DT&E Tests 1…n (Specify)
1.6.2                         Operational Test and Evaluation
1.6.2.1                                  Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation
1.6.2.2                                  Other OT&E Tests 1…n (Specify)
1.6.3                         Live Fire Test and Evaluation

1.6.5                         Test and Evaluation Support

1.7                 Training
1.9                 Peculiar Support Equipment
1.10                Common Support Equipment
1.11                Industrial Facilities
1.12                Initial Spares and Repair Parts

          M.12.1 System Test and Evaluation. The use of pilot, prototype, production, or specifically
 fabricated hardware/software to obtain or validate engineering data on the performance of the system during
 the developmental phase of the program. It also includes all effort associated with the development of any
 specialized tools or data in support of the system level test program.

          Includes, for example:
          a. Detailed planning, conduct, support, data reduction and reports (excluding the contract data
              requirements list data) from such testing, and test articles that are functionally configured to represent
              and test subsystems/components of the defense materiel end item being developed or produced
          b. Design and production of models, specimens, fixtures and instrumentation
          c. Development and/or validation of modeling and simulation resources required for Test and
              Evaluation of a specific system under test.
          d. System level labs that integrate both hardware and software
          e. Training for Government personnel to provide knowledge and capability to deliver test and
              evaluation support actions as required.

NOTE: Test articles that are functionally configured to represent and test the complete defense
materiel end item being developed or produced, are excluded from this WBS element.


          Excludes, for example:

                                                          290

                             Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                           Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                 MIL-STD-881F
                                                 APPENDIX M

         a.   All formal and informal testing up through the subsystem level, which can be associated with the
              hardware/software element acceptance testing.



NOTE: These excluded efforts are to be included with the appropriate hardware or software elements.


          M.12.1.1 Developmental Test and Evaluation. Developmental Test & Evaluation (DT&E) is
conducted throughout the acquisition process to assist in engineering design and development and to verify
that technical performance specifications have been met. DT&E is planned and monitored by the
developing agency and is normally conducted by the contractor. It includes the T&E of components,
subsystems, Preplanned Product Improvement (P3I) changes, hardware/software integration, and
production qualification testing. It encompasses the use of models, simulations, test beds, and prototypes
or full-scale engineering development models of the system.

         Includes test and evaluation conducted to:
         a. Demonstrate that the engineering design and development process is complete
         b. Demonstrate that the design risks have been minimized
         c. Demonstrate that the system will meet specifications
         d. Assess the system’s military utility when introduced
         e. Determine whether the engineering design is supportable (practical, maintainable, safe, etc.) for
              operational use
         f. Provide test data with which to examine and evaluate trade-offs against specification requirements,
              life cycle cost, and schedule
         g. Perform the logistics testing efforts to evaluate the achievement of supportability goals, the
              adequacy of the support package for the system, (e.g., deliverable maintenance tools, test
              equipment, technical publications, maintenance instructions, and personnel skills and training
              requirements, etc.)

          M.12.1.1.1 Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation. The developmental test and evaluation conducted and
monitored by the developing agency of the DoD. The technical efforts of ensuring the confidentiality,
integrity, and availability of information and technology by monitoring and defending against cyber (network)
attacks. The following DoD Guidebooks and Instruction (latest versions) should be followed for
Cybersecurity test and evaluation planning; in addition to specific Service-related guidebooks or instructions,
as appropriate.

         a.   DoD Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation Guidebook, Version 2.0
         b.   Director, Operational Test and Evaluation (DOT&E), Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP)
              Guidebook,
         c.   DoD Instruction, No. 8510.01; “Risk Management Framework (RMF) for DoD Information
              Technology (IT)”

         M.12.1.1.2 Service/Agency DT&E 1...n (Specify). The costs to rent, lease, procure, or utilize a military
weapon system in order to support the testing of a different, primary weapon system. These costs are normally
paid to by the primary weapon system program being tested to another program office/program in order to utilize
equipment or systems needed to test or characterize the primary weapon system.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Costs to utilize a ship, aircraft, ground vehicle used to act as an opposing force (e.g., “red team”).
         b. Costs to repair or demilitarize a non-primary military system damaged during the testing of the
             primary weapon system.

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Costs to procure, repair, or demilitarize the primary weapon system being tested.

         M.12.1.1.3 Other DT&E Tests 1…n (Specify). Other DT&E tests not captured elsewhere in this

                           Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                         Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                  MIL-STD-881F
                                                  APPENDIX M

commodity. The following are examples of DT&E tests that may be applicable to this commodity but were
not identified. If pertinent, include those that may be appropriate, by specifically listing them by starting with
this WBS element (M.12.1.3) NOTE: Each test should be identified by the next consecutive WBS number
(e.g., M.12.1.3.1, M.12.1.3.2, etc.).

         Examples of Developmental Tests
         a. Market Research effort (or proof of concept test) and Evaluation
         b. Technical Feasibility Test (TFT) and Evaluation
         c. Engineering development test (EDT) and Evaluation
         d. Production prove-out test (PPT) and Evaluation
         e. Production qualification Test (PQT) and Evaluation
         f. Software development test (SDT) and Evaluation
         g. Software qualification test (SQT) and Evaluation
         h. Interoperability certification test and Evaluation
         i. Soldier protection testing and Evaluation
         j. Technical Manual (TM) Verification
         k. Physical configuration audit (PCA)/Functional configuration audit (FCA)

         Examples of Pre-Operational Tests
         a. Production Verification Test (PVT) and Evaluation
         b. First Article Test (FAT) and Evaluation
         c. Comparison test
         d. Quality Conformance Inspection
         e. Lot acceptance test
         f. Post-Deployment Software Support (PDSS) Test
         g. Surveillance Test
         h. Reconditioning Test (Pilot or Initial)
         i. Control Test
         j. Acceptance Test
         k. Baseline Evaluation Test
         l. Regression Test (unique to software-intensive systems)

          M.12.1.2 Operational Test and Evaluation. The test and evaluation conducted by agencies other than
the developing command to assess the prospective system’s military utility, operational effectiveness,
operational suitability, logistics supportability (including compatibility, inter-operability, reliability,
maintainability, logistic requirements, etc.) The system is also being tested against the operational doctrine
suitable for the system against the threats expected to be encountered by the system, cost of ownership, and need
for any modifications.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Initial operational test and evaluation conducted during the development of a system
         b. Such tests as system demonstration, flight tests, sea trials, mobility demonstrations, on-orbit tests,
             spin demonstration, stability tests, qualification operational test and evaluation, etc., and support
             thereto, required to prove the operational capability of the deliverable system.
         c. Contractor support (e.g., technical assistance, maintenance, labor, material, etc.) consumed during
             this phase of testing
         d. Logistics testing efforts to evaluate the achievement of supportability goals and the adequacy of
             the support for the system (e.g., deliverable maintenance tools, test equipment, technical
             publications, maintenance instructions, personnel skills and training requirements, and software
             support facility/environment elements).
         e. Pre-full-rate production/full deployment tests - Common to all systems, including (1) Early User Test
             (EUT). (2) Limited User Test (LUT). (3) Initial Operating Test (IOT).
         f. Post-full-rate production/full deployment tests, including: (1) Full Operational Test (FOT). (2) User
             acceptance test (UAT) and unique to IT systems test.
         g. As-required tests, including (1) Customer test (CT) (common to all systems). (2) Supplemental site
             test (SST) (unique to IT systems). (3) Regression test (unique to software-intensive systems).

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                  MIL-STD-881F

          M.12.1.2.1 Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation. The operational test and evaluation conducted and
monitored by the developing agency of the DoD. The technical efforts of ensuring the confidentiality,
integrity, and availability of information and technology by monitoring and defending against cyber
(network) attacks. The following DoD Guidebooks and Instruction (latest versions) should be followed for
Cybersecurity test and evaluation planning; in addition to specific Service-related guidebooks or
instructions, as appropriate.

         a.   DoD Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation Guidebook,
         b.   Director, Operational Test and Evaluation (DOT&E), Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP)
              Guidebook,
         c.   DoD Instruction, No. 8510.01; “Risk Management Framework (RMF) for DoD Information
              Technology (IT)”

         M.12.1.2.2 Other OT&E Tests 1…n (Specify). Other OT&E tests not captured elsewhere in this
commodity. The following are examples of OT&E tests that may be applicable to this commodity but were
not identified. If pertinent, include those that may be appropriate, by specifically listing them under this WBS
element (M.12.2.2) NOTE: Each test should be identified by the next consecutive WBS number (e.g.,
M.12.2.2.1, M.12.2.2.2, etc.)

         a.   Production Verification Test (PVT) and Evaluation
         b.   First Article Test (FAT) and Evaluation
         c.   Comparison test
         d.   Quality Conformance Inspection
         e.   Lot acceptance test
         f.   Post-Deployment Software Support (PDSS) Test
         g.   Surveillance Test
         h.   Reconditioning Test (Pilot or Initial)
         i.   Control Test
         j.   Acceptance Test
         k.   Baseline Evaluation Test
         l.   Regression Test (unique to software-intensive systems.)

          M.12.1.3 Live Fire Test and Evaluation. The test and evaluation managed by the Program Office and
conducted by dedicated Service laboratories and commands to assess the system’s survivability or lethality
against all operationally representative and relevant threats or targets. It includes test and evaluation conducted
early to identify and mitigate system design vulnerabilities that could affect the mission or force protection
capabilities. It also progresses through OT&E to verify any design changes and to assess the survivability of a
production representative system prior to fielding or full-rate production (FRP). LFT&E includes component,
sub-system, and system-level testing as well as M&S, and evaluation of combat data, safety data, mishap data,
and system designs to:

         a.   Characterize and improve the ability of the system to avoid being hit during its mission. Examples
              include assessment of system signatures, active protection systems or self-defense systems intended
              to mitigate hit probabilities or manipulate hit distributions,
         b.   Identify and mitigate threat induced system design vulnerabilities to improve residual mission
              capability post attack,
         c.   Characterize threat induced crew casualties to identify and implement system design changes that
              could improve force protection in combat,
         d.   Identify and improve system design changes and procedures that could advance system
              recoverability post-combat engagement.

         M.12.1.4 Mock-ups/System Integration Labs (SILs). The design engineering and production of system
or subsystem mock-ups that have special contractual or engineering significance or that are not required solely
for the conduct of one of the above elements of testing. SILs are often used in lieu of (or in addition to) mock-
ups. SILs are risk reduction facilities where software and hardware can be developed, integrated, tested, and
evaluated for both standalone functionality and/or interoperability prior to being fielded.

                                                         293

                            Source: https://assist.dla.mil -- Downloaded: 2026-04-14T20:21Z
                          Check the source to verify that this is the current version before use.
                                                  MIL-STD-881F
                                                  APPENDIX M


         Includes, for example:
         a. Hardware/lab equipment
         b. SIL Software (written to simulate the operating environment or written to operate the SIL)

         M.12.1.5 Test and Evaluation Support. The support elements necessary to operate and maintain, during
test and evaluation, systems and subsystems, which are not consumed during the testing phase and are not
allocated to a specific phase of testing.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Repairable spares, repair of reparables, repair parts, consumables, warehousing and distribution of
              spares and repair parts, test and support equipment, test bed vehicles, drones, surveillance
              aircraft/chase aircraft, tracking vessels, contractor technical support

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Operational and maintenance personnel, special fixtures, special instrumentation, etc., that are
             utilized and/or consumed in a single element of testing and that should be included under that element
             of testing

          M.12.1.6 Test Facilities. The special test facilities required for performance of the various developmental
tests necessary to prove the design and reliability of the system or subsystem.

         Includes, for example:
         a.   Test tank test fixtures, propulsion test fixtures, white rooms, test chambers, range/targeting facilities
         b. Cost to the program of developing and/or providing the test capability.

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Brick and mortar-type facilities identified as industrial facilities
         b. Cost to the program for using the capability is not included in this WBS. The cost of providing this
             effort is associated with the WBS it supports (i.e., the actual testing effort).

         M.12.1.6.1 Commercial. Non-Government owned or operated test facilities. Note: Program offices
do pay for the equipment and for usage.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Non-Government ranges/facilities used to test missile weapon System or missile subsystem.
         b. Non-Government laboratories, part of a building, or other place equipped to conduct scientific
             experiments or tests.
         c. Other Test Facilities. Non-Government owned or operated test facilities not listed.
         M.12.1.6.2 Government (Pay-as-You-Go). Government owned or operated test facilities. Note: Program
offices do not pay for the equipment but do need to pay for usage.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Ranges - Government facilities used to test missile weapon System or missile subsystem.
         b. Laboratories - A Government building, part of a building, or other place equipped to conduct
              scientific experiments or tests.
         c. Ranges/Facilities - use of the facility, including equipment brought or provided, “man-hours”, and
              “special test equipment.”
         d. Other Test Facilities - Government owned or operated test facilities not listed.

          M.13.1.1 Developmental Test and Evaluation. Developmental Test & Evaluation (DT&E) is
conducted throughout the acquisition process to assist in engineering design and development and to verify
that technical performance specifications have been met. DT&E is planned and monitored by the
developing agency and is normally conducted by the contractor. It includes the T&E of components,
subsystems, Preplanned Product Improvement (P3I) changes, hardware/software integration, and
production qualification testing. It encompasses the use of models, simulations, test beds, and prototypes
or full-scale engineering development models of the system.

         Includes test and evaluation conducted to:
         a. Demonstrate that the engineering design and development process is complete
         b. Demonstrate that the design risks have been minimized
         c. Demonstrate that the system will meet specifications
         d. Assess the system’s military utility when introduced
         e. Determine whether the engineering design is supportable (practical, maintainable, safe, etc.) for
              operational use
         f. Provide test data with which to examine and evaluate trade-offs against specification requirements,
              life cycle cost, and schedule
         g. Perform the logistics testing efforts to evaluate the achievement of supportability goals, the
              adequacy of the support package for the system, (e.g., deliverable maintenance tools, test
              equipment, technical publications, maintenance instructions, and personnel skills and training
              requirements, etc.)

         M.13.1.1.1 System Integration Test (SIT). System integration testing is a high-level software testing
process in which testers verify that all related systems maintain data integrity and can operate in coordination with
other systems in the same environment. The testing process ensures that all subcomponents are integrated
successfully to provide expected results.

          M.13.1.1.2 System Acceptance Test. System acceptance testing is to verify in an operational environment
(either simulated or actual production) that the complete system (i.e. the full complement of application software
running on the target hardware and systems software infrastructure) satisfies specified requirements (e.g.
functional, performance, and security) and is acceptable to end users.

         M.13.1.1.3 System Reliability Test. Burn-in testing, regression testing, etc. to test the system within the
intended operationally relevant mission environment,

          M.13.1.1.4 Environmental Testing. Objective testing that provides the system can operate in austere
environments (heat, cold, shake) by using the following: calibrated environment chamber, outside testing, etc.,
prior to fielding.

          M.13.1.1.5 Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation. The developmental test and evaluation conducted
and monitored by the developing agency of the DoD. The technical efforts of ensuring the confidentiality,
integrity, and availability of information and technology by monitoring and defending against cyber
(network) attacks. The following DoD Guidebooks and Instruction (latest versions) should be followed for
Cybersecurity test and evaluation planning; in addition to specific Service-related guidebooks or
instructions, as appropriate.

         a.   DoD Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation Guidebook,
         b.   Director, Operational Test and Evaluation (DOT&E), Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP)

              Guidebook.
         c.   Army Regulation 73-1 Test and Evaluation Policy
         d.   DoD Instruction, No. 8510.01; “Risk Management Framework (RMF) for DoD Information
              Technology (IT)”

         M.13.1.1.6 Service/Agency DT&E 1...n (Specify) The costs to rent, lease, procure, or utilize a military

         Includes, for example:
         a. Costs to utilize a ship, aircraft, ground vehicle used to act as an opposing force (e.g., “red team”).
         b. Costs to repair or demilitarize a non-primary military system damaged during the testing of the
             primary weapon system.

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Costs to procure, repair, or demilitarize the primary weapon system being tested.

operational suitability, logistics supportability (including compatibility, inter-operability, reliability,
maintainability, logistic requirements, etc.) The system is also being tested against the operational doctrine
suitable for the system against the threats expected to be encountered by the system, cost of ownership, and need
for any modifications.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Initial operational test and evaluation conducted during the development of a system

         b.   Such tests as system demonstration, flight tests, sea trials, mobility demonstrations, on-orbit tests, spin
              demonstration, stability tests, qualification operational test and evaluation, etc., and support thereto,
              required to prove the operational capability of the deliverable system.
         c.   Contractor support (e.g., technical assistance, maintenance, labor, material, etc.) consumed during this
              phase of testing
         d.   Logistics testing efforts to evaluate the achievement of supportability goals and the adequacy of the
              support for the system (e.g., deliverable maintenance tools, test equipment, technical publications,

         e.   Pre-full-rate production/full deployment tests - Common to all systems, including (1) Early User Test

         f.   Post-full-rate production/full deployment tests, including: (1) Full Operational Test (FOT). (2) User

         g.   As-required tests, including (1) Customer test (CT) (common to all systems). (2) Supplemental site test
              (SST) (unique to IT systems). (3) Regression test (unique to software-intensive systems).
         M.13.1.2.1 Limited User Evaluation (LUE/M-LUT/FUE). Any type of RDTE funded user test conducted
that does not address all the effectiveness, suitability, and survivability issues and is therefore limited in comparison
to an IOT that must address all effectiveness, suitability, and survivability issues. The Limited User Test (LUT)
addresses a limited number of operational issues.
        M.13.1.2.2 Interoperability Testing (IOP with IA/OT&E). The process of assessing the ability of a system
to exchange usable electronic information with systems of other services or nations as specified in its requirements
documents.

          M.13.1.2.3 Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation. The operational test and evaluation conducted and
monitored by the developing agency of the DoD. The technical efforts of ensuring the confidentiality, integrity, and
availability of information and technology by monitoring and defending against cyber (network) attacks. The
following DoD Guidebooks and Instruction (latest versions) should be followed for Cybersecurity test and evaluation
planning; in addition to specific Service-related guidebooks or instructions, as appropriate.

         a.   DoD Cybersecurity Test and Evaluation Guidebook, Version 2
         b.   Director, Operational Test and Evaluation (DOT&E), Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP)
              Guidebook.
         c.   Army Regulation 73-1 Test and Evaluation Policy
         d.   DoD Instruction, No. 8510.01; “Risk Management Framework (RMF) for DoD Information
              Technology (IT)”

         M.13.1.2.4 Other OT&E Tests 1…n (Specify) Other OT&E tests not captured elsewhere in this commodity.
The following are examples of OT&E tests that may be applicable to this commodity but were not identified. If
pertinent, include those that may be appropriate, by specifically listing them under this WBS element (M.13.2.4)
NOTE: Each test should be identified by the next consecutive WBS number (e.g., M.13.2.4.1, M.13.2.4.2, etc.)

                                                 APPENDIX M

or subsystem mock-ups that have special contractual or engineering significance or that are not required solely
for the conduct of one of the above elements of testing. SILs are often used in lieu of (or in addition to) mock-
ups. SILs are risk reduction facilities where software and hardware can be developed, integrated, tested, and
evaluated for both standalone functionality and/or interoperability prior to being fielded.

         M.13.1.4 Test and Evaluation Management / Support. The support elements necessary to operate and
maintain, during test and evaluation, systems and subsystems, which are not consumed during the testing phase
and are not allocated to a specific phase of testing.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Repairable spares, repair of reparables, repair parts, consumables, warehousing and distribution of
             spares and repair parts, test and support equipment, test bed vehicles, drones, surveillance
             aircraft/chase aircraft, tracking vessels, contractor technical support

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Operational and maintenance personnel, special fixtures, special instrumentation, etc., that are
             utilized and/or consumed in a single element of testing and that should be included under that element
             of testing

         M.13.1.5 Test Facilities. The special test facilities required for performance of the various developmental
or operational tests necessary to prove the design and reliability of the system or subsystem.

         Includes, for example:
         a. Test tank test fixtures, propulsion test fixtures, white rooms, test chambers, range/targeting facilities

         Excludes, for example:
         a. Brick and mortar-type facilities identified elsewhere as industrial facilities

         M.13.1.5.1 Commercial. Non-Government owned or operated test facilities. Note: Program offices
do pay for the equipment and for usage.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Non-Government ranges/facilities used to test missile weapon System or missile subsystem.
         b. Non-Government laboratories, part of a building, or other place equipped to conduct scientific
             experiments or tests.
         c. Other Test Facilities. Non-Government owned or operated test facilities not listed.
         M.13.1.5.2 Government (Pay-as-You-Go). Government owned or operated test facilities. Note: Program
offices do not pay for the equipment but do need to pay for usage.
         Includes, for example:
         a. Ranges - Government facilities used to test missile weapon System or missile subsystem.
         b. Laboratories - A Government building, part of a building, or other place equipped to conduct
              scientific experiments or tests.
         c. Ranges/Facilities - use of the facility, including equipment brought or provided, “man-hours”, and
              “special test equipment.”
         d. Other Test Facilities - Government owned or operated test facilities not listed.
